Akhmet Khan.age: deceased before main timeline; was a contemporary of Hadji Murad — likely 40s–50s at time of death
Akhmet Khan.origin: Avaria, North Caucasus; Avar khan, native Caucasian aristocracy
Akhmet Khan.social_status: Avar khan, briefly appointed Khan of Kazi-Kumukh by Russian general Rosen; aligned with the Russian colonial administration against rival native lineages
Akhmet Khan.want: To consolidate power over Avaria by eliminating Hadji Murad and securing his bloodline's claim through the marriage of his son to Sultanetta, daughter of the Khansha.
Akhmet Khan.need: Recognition as the legitimate heir to Avar authority — to be feared by his own people, not merely tolerated by Russian generals.
Akhmet Khan.fear: That Hadji Murad — younger, braver, more beloved by the mountain people — will outshine and ultimately replace him in the eyes of both the Russians and the Avars.
Akhmet Khan.contradiction: Serves the Russian Empire to crush a fellow Caucasian, yet stakes his legitimacy on traditional Avar dynastic marriage and bloodline — uses colonial machinery to defend a tribal throne.
Akhmet Khan.justification: Hadji Murad is a turbaned upstart who blocked my son's marriage and courts Shamil behind Russian backs — denouncing him is the only way to protect my house and the Avar khanate from a man who serves no master but himself.

arc.act_1_starting_state: Established Avar rival in HM's backstory (delivered via flashback / Loris-Melikov interview): an entrenched khan who has just been bested politically by HM in the matter of Sultanetta's marriage; nurses a fresh humiliation.
arc.act_2_change: Acts on the grievance — denounces HM to General Klugenau over the turban affair, triggering HM's arrest and ultimate defection to Shamil; from this single act of calumny the entire main plot unfolds. Dies offstage (in backstory) before HM's return, leaving his son to inherit the feud.
arc.act_3_end_state: Present only as legacy — his son rides in the final assault wave alongside Hadji Aga to kill HM, completing the vendetta the father began. The dead khan's hand reaches into the present through inherited blood-debt.
arc.arc_type: Stagnation (within his own lifespan) / Revelation (for the audience: the betrayal that began everything is finally named)

voice_profile.voice_description: Akhmet Khan speaks in the measured, formal register of a Caucasian khan addressing equals and superiors — short declarative sentences with the weight of pronouncement. His speech is courteous on the surface and corrosive underneath; he prefers to wound through report and accusation rather than direct insult. In the few flashback lines he is granted, he speaks of HM in the third person even when HM is present, as if HM has already been judged and is no longer worth addressing.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: formal, aristocratic, religiously inflected (Quranic and customary Avar idiom); educated in the oral-political tradition of khans, not in Russian letters — speaks Russian with stiff correctness when forced
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium sentences (8–14 words), heavily declarative, low question-rate. Frequent parallel constructions and triadic phrases typical of Caucasian rhetorical speech. Almost never uses fragments — every utterance is a complete pronouncement.
verbal_tics: Refers to HM as 'that man' or 'the son of Hitsl Mahoma' rather than by name
Opens accusations with 'Let it be known...' or 'I have seen...'
Closes statements with appeals to honor or to 'the law of the mountains'
Uses 'turban' and 'oath' as loaded keywords against rivals
avoidances: Never admits envy or personal grievance — always frames attacks as duty to Russia or to Avar custom
Never uses casual or affectionate diminutives
Never jokes; humor is beneath the dignity of his station
Never apologizes or qualifies an accusation once made
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Suppresses all visible feeling beneath formal courtesy; rage emerges as colder formality and longer pauses, never as a raised voice. Pleasure shows only as the briefest tightening at the corner of the mouth. Reports an enemy's downfall with the same flat tone he would use to announce the weather.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, slow, weighted — each clause lands like a verdict; longer pauses between sentences than the listener expects

example_lines.line: The roads from Khunzakh are open. My riders went out at dawn and returned before noon.
General, I have served the Tsar from the day Rosen placed his hand on my shoulder. If my word is doubted now, then doubt every Avar who wears your epaulettes.
When my son takes Sultanetta to wife, the line of the Khansha and the line of my house will be one — and Avaria will sleep in one bed.
Let it be known: that man has wound a turban around his cap and rides at night to Shamil. He teaches the villages to spit on the Tsar's hand. Arrest him, or I will.
I am not loved as he is loved. I know this. But I have not run, and I will not run — and that, in the end, is what a khan owes his land.

subtext_rules.when_says_the_law_of_the_mountains_means: my interpretation, which happens to favor my house
subtext_rules.when_praises_a_rival_means: is preparing the ground to denounce them
subtext_rules.when_silent_means: the verdict is already passed; the words are only delayed

Hadji Murad.quality: lethal, asymmetric — Akhmet Khan envies HM's charisma and military reputation; HM regards Akhmet Khan as a liar whose denunciation cost him his standing with the Russians and forced him to Shamil
Hadji Murad.power_dynamic: Akhmet Khan holds the bureaucratic ear of the Russian generals; HM holds the loyalty of the mountain warriors — cold equilibrium that Akhmet breaks first by calumny
Hadji Murad.speech_register_shift: Refuses to address HM directly — speaks of him in the third person even within earshot; with HM absent, speech becomes more clinical and precise

General Klugenau.quality: instrumental — Klugenau is the conduit through which Akhmet Khan converts personal hatred into Russian state action against HM
General Klugenau.power_dynamic: subordinate in form, manipulator in fact — feeds Klugenau the framing that triggers HM's arrest
General Klugenau.speech_register_shift: stiff Russian, exaggerated deference, every accusation framed as service to the Tsar

Khansha / Sultanetta.quality: humiliation source — the Khansha refused Sultanetta's hand for Akhmet Khan's son, which he believes HM engineered
Khansha / Sultanetta.power_dynamic: outranked socially by the Khansha's lineage; the refusal is the wound that festers into the denunciation of HM
Khansha / Sultanetta.speech_register_shift: in their presence, exaggerated courtly formality that barely masks contempt

His son (rides with Hadji Aga in final a.quality: the inheritance is not land but the unfinished feud with HM
His son (rides with Hadji Aga in final a.power_dynamic: father instructs in absentia — the son's presence at HM's killing closes a circle the father opened
His son (rides with Hadji Aga in final a.speech_register_shift: instructional, paternal — the only register in which Akhmet Khan permits warmth

Shamil.quality: Akhmet Khan represents the Russian-collaborating khan; Shamil represents the resisting imam — Akhmet uses Shamil's name as the ultimate accusation against HM
Shamil.power_dynamic: no direct contact in text; weaponizes Shamil-association as a denunciation tool
Shamil.speech_register_shift: speaks Shamil's name with theatrical disgust, especially when Russian officers can hear

fusion_decision.rationale: Akhmet Khan is the historically anchored trigger of the entire main plot — his denunciation of HM over the turban is the direct cause of HM's defection to Shamil and thus of the film's premise; collapsing him into Hadji Aga or Mahomet-Murza would erase the named originator of HM's tragedy and conflate two distinct narrative functions (backstory-betrayer vs. present-day executioner-kunak), violating M-03 (historically anchored) and the figurenfusion rule that fusion requires identical dramatic function.

Hamzad.name: Hamzad
Hamzad.initial_description: Historical Imam predecessor of Shamil; killed HM's family — anchors HM's blood-feud backstory and explains his defection logic toward the Russians.

Hamzad.age: Adult male, contemporary of Kazi-Mulla; succeeds him as Imam (historical: 1832–1834)
Hamzad.origin: Caucasus highlands, Avar region; militant Naqshbandi Sufi lineage
Hamzad.social_status: Imam — religious-military supreme leader of the Caucasian resistance, predecessor of Shamil
Hamzad.want: Force the Avar Khans of Khunzakh into the Ghazavat (holy war) against Russia and consolidate religious-military authority over the entire Caucasus by eliminating the Khan dynasty that refuses to submit.
Hamzad.need: Submission of all rivals to the Imamate as a single political-religious body — without that, the Ghazavat cannot be waged; he believes God's law requires unity even at the price of treachery.
Hamzad.fear: That the Khans' alliance with the Russians will fracture the resistance and that the highlands will fall to the infidel before the Ghazavat is consolidated.
Hamzad.contradiction: Invokes sacred hospitality (receives the Khansha's son Bulach as hostage 'well') and then violates the deepest highland code by murdering guests inside his own tent — religious zeal weaponized against the very honor-system it claims to defend.
Hamzad.justification: The Khans bargained with the infidel and threatened the Ghazavat; under God's law their lives were already forfeit, and a single treacherous act in my tent saves a thousand believers from Russian chains.

arc.starting_state: Successor to the martyred Imam Kazi-Mulla; sends envoys to demand the Khans join the Ghazavat or face destruction.
arc.change: Receives the Khansha's youngest son Bulach as hostage in apparent good faith, lures Umma Khan and Abu Nutsal into his tent under the guise of teaching the Ghazavat, has them killed in his own quarters, then rides into Khunzakh and orders the Khansha struck down by his murid Aseldar.
arc.end_state: Has annihilated the Khan dynasty of Khunzakh and seized their palace — but the act of treachery seals the blood-feud that drives Hadji Murat's lifelong arc and ultimately Shamil's downfall through Hadji Murat's defection.
arc.arc_type: Fall (off-stage; his triumph is the seed of the resistance's later fracture)
arc.narrative_function: Backstory-Antagonist: never appears live in the screenplay's present timeline — referenced in Hadji Murat's recounted past (chunk_048–055) and once as a song heard the night before HM goes to fetch water (chunk_114), foreshadowing HM's own death.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hamzad speaks the clipped, absolute idiom of a religious commander who treats every conversation as a verdict. His voice carries the formal weight of Quranic citation but never wastes a syllable on warmth. When he must lie — to the Khansha, to Bulach, to Umma Khan in his tent — his courtesy is cold marble, perfectly polite, perfectly hollow.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, religiously coded, archaic-prophetic register; mixes Sufi/Quranic vocabulary (Ghazavat, sheik, murid, infidel) with terse military commands; education is religious-juridical rather than literary.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short declaratives and ultimatums. Average 6–10 words. Few questions — he issues, he does not ask. Rare conditional clauses, all of them threats ('if they do not join... I will destroy').
verbal_tics: 'God's law' / 'the law of the Prophet' as legitimation prefix
'the Ghazavat' invoked as the single non-negotiable horizon
names rivals by clan-and-position rather than personal name (distancing)
uses hospitality formulas immediately before lethal acts ('be at peace in my tent')
avoidances: personal affection or familial warmth — would never use a diminutive
doubt or qualification ('perhaps', 'maybe', 'I think')
humor of any kind
Russian loanwords (would mark him as compromised)
any apology or admission of treachery — frames every act as God's verdict
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Suppressed and theological. Anger becomes scripture; ambition becomes duty; even the kill order is delivered as a wink to the murid Aseldar — emotion offloaded onto a subordinate's hand.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, slow, weighted. Pauses are deliberate; each sentence lands like a ruling. Accelerates only when issuing the final command.

example_dialog_lines.neutral_everyday: Sit. Eat. The sheik will come at sundown.
example_dialog_lines.under_pressure: The Khans have one road left. The Ghazavat — or the grave.
example_dialog_lines.hopeful_joyful: Send me your youngest son. I will receive him as my own — and the highlands will be one hand, one sword.
example_dialog_lines.angry_hurt: You bargain with the infidel and you speak to me of honor? God already wrote your sentence.
example_dialog_lines.vulnerable_honest: I do not weep for them. I weep that the law required it.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_consider_it_he_means: you have until sundown to surrender or die

Kazi-Mulla.quality: Reverent — Kazi-Mulla's death legitimates Hamzad's succession
Kazi-Mulla.power_dynamic: Inherits authority; speaks of him only to claim continuity

Shamil.quality: Trusted lieutenant who will inherit the Imamate after Hamzad's own assassination (historical, off-screen for HM's narrative)
Shamil.power_dynamic: Hamzad above; Shamil below but rising
Shamil.register_shift: Commanding, doctrinal — treats Shamil as instrument of the Ghazavat

Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).type: Targeted enemy under the mask of negotiation
Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).quality: Cold strategic courtesy followed by murder by proxy
Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).power_dynamic: Hamzad seizes total power once her sons are dead; she is left alive only long enough to be silenced
Khansha (mother of the Avar Khans).register_shift: Honorific address ('mother of the Khans') used as cover; the kill order delivered wordlessly via a wink to Aseldar

Umma Khan & Abu Nutsal.quality: Lured into his tent under pretense of teaching the Ghazavat, then killed
Umma Khan & Abu Nutsal.power_dynamic: Inversion — guest-status weaponized; Hamzad violates the highland sacred bond of the host
Umma Khan & Abu Nutsal.register_shift: Warm hospitality formulas immediately before the order is given

Bulach Khan (youngest son, hostage).quality: Received 'well' — performance of trust to extract the larger family
Bulach Khan (youngest son, hostage).power_dynamic: Total — the child's life as collateral

Aseldar (his murid).quality: Wordless trust — Hamzad winks, Aseldar strikes the Khansha down
Aseldar (his murid).power_dynamic: Master-instrument; violence delegated so Hamzad's own hand stays ritually clean
Aseldar (his murid).register_shift: No words at all — the relationship runs on gesture

Hadji Murat (off-stage, structural).quality: They never meet on-screen, but Hamzad's slaughter of HM's family (and the Khans HM served) is the foundational wound that drives HM's entire blood-feud arc and his eventual defection to the Russians against Shamil
Hadji Murat (off-stage, structural).power_dynamic: Posthumous — Hamzad shapes HM's life from the grave; HM's revenge logic is set in motion by Hamzad's act
Hadji Murat (off-stage, structural).register_shift: Hamzad is referenced in HM's recounted past and in a song HM hears the night before he dies (chunk_114) — Hamzad's name foreshadows HM's own end

fusion_decision.rationale: Hamzad is a historically anchored Imam (1832–1834), the named predecessor of Shamil and the agent of a specific recounted atrocity (the Khunzakh massacre) that supplies Hadji Murat's blood-feud motivation and defection logic; like Shamil and Vorontsov he is an anchor figure and cannot be fused without erasing the causal chain that makes HM's switch to the Russians intelligible.

"It's all about the same matter. ... Il a eu quelques desagrements avec le commandant de la place.  Simon a eu tort. ... But 'All's well...

"It's all about the same matter. ... Il a eu quelques desagrements avec le commandant de la place.  Simon a eu tort. ... But 'All's well...
Meller-Zakomelsky.origin: Russian Imperial military aristocracy (Baltic-German surname pattern)
Meller-Zakomelsky.social_status: General, regional commanding officer in the Caucasus theater; outranks Vorontsov nominally but holds less real influence
Meller-Zakomelsky.want: To be properly recognized and reported to as the ranking commander — to have protocol observed in his jurisdiction.
Meller-Zakomelsky.need: To accept that formal rank without political weight is hollow; that the empire respects influence, not titles.
Meller-Zakomelsky.fear: Being publicly outranked or bypassed by a subordinate (Vorontsov) whose court connections eclipse his own command authority.
Meller-Zakomelsky.contradiction: Insists on the prerogatives of command while privately knowing — and being known to know — that his commands carry less weight than Vorontsov's suggestions.

arc.act_1: Off-screen authority figure; learns of Hadji Murat's surrender after the fact and is publicly displeased that protocol was breached.
arc.act_2: Summons Hadji Murat through his aide-de-camp to assert jurisdiction; the encounter exposes that real power flows around him to Vorontsov.
arc.act_3: Recedes into bureaucratic background — referenced in correspondence as a formality already smoothed over ('Simon a eu tort... All's well that ends well').

voice_profile.voice_description: Bureaucratic-military register: clipped, formal, weighted with rank-language and regulatory phrasing. Speaks in declarative orders and procedural reproaches rather than questions; uses passive constructions to depersonalize friction ('it has not been reported'). Tone is cold-correct, never warm — the voice of a man who confuses protocol with presence.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal, educated, military-administrative; mid-19th-century Russian officer-class register with occasional French interjections of the court ('desagrements', 'Simon a eu tort')
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Medium-to-long declarative sentences; subordinate clauses for protocol citation; questions are rare and rhetorical; fragments only as terse commands
verbal_tics: 'It has been brought to my attention' / 'I have been informed'
Reflexive citation of rank ('as commanding officer')
'I require...' rather than 'I would like...'
Closes orders with a clipped 'That will be all.'
avoidances: Personal warmth or humor
Admitting Vorontsov's superior influence aloud
Casual or first-name address with subordinates
Showing curiosity about Hadji Murat as a man rather than a file
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Displeasure shown through procedural language and silence — never raised voice. A cleared throat, a fixed stare, a slow signature on a document. Approval is granted by absence of reproof.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, deliberate, with short pauses where a less rank-conscious man would soften the line

example_lines.neutral: The aide-de-camp will read the dispatch. I will sign it after.
example_lines.under_pressure: It has not been reported to me. I am the commanding officer in this district. I require the prisoner brought before me by morning.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: The matter is closed. Protocol has been observed. That will be all, gentlemen.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Prince Vorontsov has chosen to inform Tiflis before he informs his own commander. I will note it in the record.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: A general's signature is not always a general's command. One learns this. One does not say it aloud.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_protocol: He means: my rank is being ignored.
subtext_rules.when_he_cites_regulations: He means: I have no other lever.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_'that_will_be_all': He means: I will not let you see I have already lost this.
subtext_rules.when_he_praises_a_junior_officer: He means: I am marking you as compliant for future use.

Vorontsov.type: Nominal subordinate / actual rival in influence
Vorontsov.quality: Cold-correct, surface-cordial, undercut by jurisdictional resentment
Vorontsov.power_dynamic: Meller-Zakomelsky outranks formally; Vorontsov outweighs him in court influence — a humiliation Meller-Zakomelsky must absorb in protocol-language
Vorontsov.speech_register_shift: Formal third-person address, French phrases borrowed from court register; never the warm familiarity Vorontsov uses with peers

Hadji Murat.quality: Procedural, depersonalized; treats him as a jurisdictional question, not a man
Hadji Murat.power_dynamic: Total formal authority, zero personal interest
Hadji Murat.speech_register_shift: Speaks about him in the third person even when present; uses interpreter rather than direct address

Aide-de-camp.quality: Strictly hierarchical; the aide is voice and conduit, not interlocutor

Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).type: Junior officer who 'erred' by not reporting first
Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).quality: Reproachful but ultimately deferential to the family name
Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).power_dynamic: Formal superior who must let the matter pass once the father intervenes
Simon Vorontsov (Vorontsov's son).speech_register_shift: Reproof framed as procedural correction, not personal anger

fusion_decision.rationale: Both are mid-tier Russian generals functioning as bureaucratic chain-of-custody figures over Hadji Murat with no independent dramatic action; collapsing them into a single Composite-Russian-Frontline-Officer (canonical: Kozlovsky) preserves the jurisdictional-friction beat (offended by not being informed first) while eliminating a redundant rank-tier per CF-01 and CF-09.

Major Petrov.name: Major Petrov
Major Petrov.initial_description: Russian fort major in the Chechen frontier sectors; embodies routine Russian command at the boundary of empire.
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_079: ...agers are, Fagers are!"  Butler rode beside the officer next in rank above him, Major Petrov, with whom he lived, and he felt he could not be thankful enough to have exchanged from the Guards and come to the Caucasus.  His chief reason for exchanging was that he had lost all he had at cards and was...

Chunk chunk_107: ...hich the regimental singers performed and which ended up with a drinking bout.  Major Petrov, very drunk and no longer red but ash pale, sat astride a chair and, drawing his sword, hacked at imaginary foes, alternately swearing and laughi...

Chunk chunk_108: ...Major Petrov, very drunk and no longer red but ash pale, sat astride a chair and, drawing his sword, hacked at imaginary foes, alternately swearing and laughing, now embracing someone and now dancing to the tune of his f...
Major Petrov.age: mid-40s (estimated from rank and bearing; not stated explicitly)
Major Petrov.origin: Russian provincial gentry; long service on the Caucasian frontier
Major Petrov.social_status: Russian Imperial Army Major, fort commander on the Chechen line; married to Marya Dmitrievna; lives in spartan officer quarters within the fort
Major Petrov.want: To keep his small frontier sector running by routine — patrols, ambushes, evening drink, regimental songs — and to be seen as a competent commander by the men who live around his table.
Major Petrov.need: To feel that his life on the frontier still has shape and meaning; underneath the routine he needs the dignity that years of war and provincial obscurity have eroded.
Major Petrov.fear: Public collapse — that the next drinking bout reveals he is no longer in command of himself, his marriage, or his post.
Major Petrov.contradiction: He gives orders by day and unravels by night; the same authority that holds the fort together dissolves into sword-waving farce at his own table.

arc.act1_start: Steady fort-major: Butler rides beside him on patrols, lives under his roof, accepts his hospitality as the natural order of frontier life.
arc.act2_change: The evenings curdle — songs, vodka, the famous drinking bout where Petrov hacks at imaginary foes with his sword; the routine that defined him begins to corrode the household and his wife Marya Dmitrievna.
arc.act3_end: Stagnation in defeat: he remains in command, unchanged in rank, but the fort scenes have shown that the Russian frontier order is held together by men who are themselves coming apart.

voice_profile.voice_description: Major Petrov speaks in short, blunt military sentences with a provincial Russian flavour, the cadence of an officer who has given the same orders for fifteen years. Sober, his voice is flat, declarative, slightly nasal; drunk, it slides into singsong, repetition, and sudden shouts that crack the room. He prefers commands and toasts to conversation and almost never finishes a sentimental thought.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Informal-formal hybrid: military-formal in duty contexts (orders, reports), provincial-informal at table; modest education, frontier slang, no French ornament; era-appropriate 1850s Russian officer register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 6–10 words sober; under drink, fragments and exclamations of 2–4 words; heavy on imperatives and statements, almost no questions except rhetorical ones
verbal_tics: Brothers — at the start of toasts and rebukes
Well then — as a transition before any decision
By God — as filler oath, not blasphemy
Pour — repeated as shorthand for the whole evening's program
Enough — to cut off both subordinates and his own wife
avoidances: Never speaks of his marriage in front of other officers
Never admits fear of the Chechens or of his own men
Never quotes literature, French, or court manners
Never apologises after a drunken scene — pretends it did not happen
Never speaks tenderly to Marya Dmitrievna in public
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Emotion comes out sideways: as a louder order, a harder drink, a sudden embrace of whoever is nearest, or a sword drawn against an imaginary enemy. Affection is gruff and physical — a hand on the shoulder, a refilled glass — never named.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured and clipped on duty; alternating to hectic and looping under drink, with long silences between bursts

example_lines.neutral_everyday: Butler. Saddle up. We ride the line at first light — same as yesterday, same as tomorrow.
example_lines.under_pressure: Hold the gate. Two men on the wall, the rest with me. No firing until I say. By God, no firing until I say.
example_lines.hopeful_joyful: Brothers — the song! Pour, pour, fill them properly. Tonight the fort drinks like men, not like clerks.
example_lines.angry_hurt: Enough. Sit down. You think you know this country better than I do? I've buried better officers than you on this line.
example_lines.vulnerable_honest: Marya. Don't look at me like that. The frontier eats a man slowly. I'm still here, aren't I. I'm still here.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_pour_more: He means: do not let me notice what this evening is becoming.
subtext_rules.when_he_says_routine_patrol: He means: I am still in command of something, even if only of a road.
subtext_rules.when_he_shouts_at_imaginary_foes: He means: the real enemies — boredom, his marriage, his own decline — cannot be cut down with a sword.
subtext_rules.when_he_calls_Butler_brother: He means: stay close, you are the only witness who still sees me as a soldier and not as a drunk.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_with_Marya_Dmitriev: He means: I know what I am doing to this house, and I have no language to stop it.

Well then — as a transition before any decision
avoidances: Never speaks of his marriage in front of other…
Never admits fear of the Chechens or of his own…
voice_profile_compact.example_line: Butler. Saddle up. We ride the line at first light — same as yesterday, same as tomorrow.

Butler.quality: Warm, gruff, paternal; Butler lives under his roof and rides beside him
Butler.power_dynamic: Petrov is clearly above in rank and in domestic authority, but Butler's youth and Guards background give him a moral leverage Petrov half-resents and half-relies on
Butler.speech_register_shift: With Butler alone: more relaxed, near-confessional in fragments; in front of other officers: clipped, commanding, performs the senior role

Marya Dmitrievna.quality: Long-eroded affection encased in routine; she is the caretaker of his dissolution
Marya Dmitrievna.power_dynamic: Public: he commands; private: she manages him, his drinking, the household, and increasingly the fort's social fabric — a quiet inversion
Marya Dmitrievna.speech_register_shift: In public: terse, almost formal, as if she were a fellow officer; in private: shorter still, often only a name, a sigh, a single imperative

Regimental officers / soldiers.quality: Bonded by ritual — patrol, song, vodka — not by intimacy
Regimental officers / soldiers.power_dynamic: Unquestioned authority on duty; on drinking nights the hierarchy dissolves into shared chaos that he leads from the front
Regimental officers / soldiers.speech_register_shift: Duty: imperatives, surnames, no warmth; drink: 'brothers,' first names, toasts, embraces

Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.type: Distant counterpart — the famed Avar guest in the Russian sector
Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.quality: Curiosity tempered by professional wariness; Petrov registers HM as a phenomenon, not a peer
Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.power_dynamic: Formally Petrov is host and Russian authority; in human stature HM dwarfs him, which Petrov senses and covers with extra brusqueness
Hadji Murat (if encountered in fort scen.speech_register_shift: Stiffer, more formal, fewer tics; the drunken Petrov never appears in HM's presence

fusion_decision.rationale: Petrov is the load-bearing commandant figure of the Butler / fort-life vignette (Section 22 strand) and the husband whose dissolution gives Marya Dmitrievna her dramatic ground; fusing him with Poltoratsky, Bogdanovich, or Serogin would either flatten the frontier-rot theme into one generic officer or destroy the only domestic Russian household in the script.

data.type: former imam and patron, now mortal enemy and captor of his family
data.quality: reverence corroded into hatred; bond of oath turned to bond of vendetta
data.power_dynamic: Shamil holds his family — absolute leverage; HM holds his own legend, which Shamil cannot replace
data.register_shift: speaks of him only with controlled formality, never by curse; the restraint is itself the measure of the rage

data.quality: mutual respect between two professionals of power; warmth without trust
data.power_dynamic: Vorontsov has armies, treasure, and time; HM has a name Vorontsov wants to display
data.register_shift: most ceremonious register — full titles, slow tempo, careful translation; HM gives him the warrior's courtesy due a worthy adversary

data.quality: quiet courtesy; she sees the man, not the trophy; he registers it
data.power_dynamic: she has no political power but moral perception; he is briefly disarmed
data.register_shift: softer, shorter sentences; gifts instead of arguments; the only register where he comes close to gentleness

data.quality: the only Russian to whom he gives the chronological truth of his past
data.power_dynamic: near-equal in the room of memory; Loris listens, HM speaks
data.register_shift: longer answers, more first-person narrative, occasional Avar phrases left untranslated — the closest he comes to confession

data.quality: absolute trust; minimal words required
data.power_dynamic: patron and disciple; HM commands by glance
data.register_shift: fewest words of any relationship — orders in two-word fragments, often only a nod

data.type: son and central love-object of the want-line
data.quality: absolute, mostly unspoken; the engine of every decision
data.power_dynamic: the boy is the lever every other character uses against him
data.register_shift: Yusuf's name is rationed; when spoken, the voice drops — the rarest and heaviest register

data.quality: lifelong tension between her warning ('do not be Khan, do not be killed') and his life as both
data.power_dynamic: she has only memory and song; that is enough to haunt him
data.register_shift: invoked only in private or near death; the one register that admits failure

data.quality: courteous distance; HM treats him kindly but knows he is a guard
data.power_dynamic: HM is older, more dangerous, formally lower in rank — a Caucasian inversion
data.register_shift: patient, almost paternal tone; teaches by example rather than instruction

data.type: distant sovereign / abstract guarantor of the bargain
data.quality: never met; an idea HM invokes for leverage and discards in private
data.power_dynamic: absolute on paper, useless in practice; HM's family is not in St Petersburg
data.register_shift: the most formal title-laden phrasing in public, complete silence about him in private

data.quality: Outwardly warm, genuinely curious, fundamentally instrumental; admires Hadji Murad as one admires a rare animal one intends to keep
data.power_dynamic: Total — Vorontsov controls movement, communication, and ransom; Hadji Murad's freedom is a fiction Vorontsov maintains for both their dignities
data.speech_register_shift: Slows further, softens further, French recedes; he speaks almost intimately, as if to a brother-in-arms — which makes the betrayal subtler and deeper

data.quality: Respectful partnership; she is one of the few people to whom he reveals genuine fatigue or doubt
data.power_dynamic: Equal in private, public asymmetry observed in form only
data.speech_register_shift: Drops court formality; uses her name and patronymic, allows fragments and sighs, may admit moral ambivalence

data.quality: Loyal, deferential, strategically careful; Vorontsov knows the Tsar distrusts independent prestige and writes accordingly
data.power_dynamic: Total subordination upward; Vorontsov bows even on paper
data.speech_register_shift: Letters become stately and impersonal; the lyrical Vorontsov disappears into court formula

data.quality: Cordial, encouraging, occasionally testing them with a soft question that has a sharp answer hidden inside
data.power_dynamic: Absolute, but exercised through suggestion, not command
data.speech_register_shift: Adds 'mon cher' and first names; becomes slightly playful when satisfied, fractionally cooler when displeased

data.quality: Performed hospitality; he ensures their comfort to the centimetre while his officers watch every door
data.power_dynamic: Total but hidden behind ceremony
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks through interpreters with elaborate courtesy, choosing nouns that flatter their honor while binding their movement

data.type: former master and best warrior turned defector / mortal enemy
data.quality: intimate adversarial — Shamil knows HM's body, courage, and pride better than HM's wife does; reads his defection as personal betrayal disguised as politics
data.power_dynamic: Shamil holds HM's family — absolute leverage; HM holds Shamil's reputation hostage to his survival
data.speech_register_shift: When speaking of HM in council: grave, almost reverent ('the lion who has lost his way'). When speaking of HM in private: clipped, dry, weary. Never contemptuous in public — contempt would diminish Shamil's loss.

character_relationships.to: Hadji Murad's mother / wife / sons (held hostage in Vedeno)

data.with: Hadji Murad's mother / wife / sons (held hostage in Vedeno)
data.quality: ceremonial guardianship masking strategic captivity; treats them with formal correctness to preserve sharia-legal cover
data.power_dynamic: absolute — their lives are paragraphs in his letters to HM
data.speech_register_shift: Refers to them by formal kinship terms ('the mother of Yussuf', 'the wife of the deserter'); never by personal names in public

data.type: feudal-religious vassals — half disciples, half rival warlords
data.quality: watchful suspicion under sacred fraternity; their loyalty is conditional on his successes
data.power_dynamic: Shamil dominant via charisma + sharia + executions, but each naib is a potential second Hadji Murad
data.speech_register_shift: Public: liturgical, distant, third-person Imam. Private with most-trusted: still measured but slightly drier, allowing the human to peek through

data.type: spiritual sons; instruments of his will
data.quality: absolute devotion expected and given; he is their tariqa-master
data.power_dynamic: absolute and uncontested
data.speech_register_shift: Shortest, most clipped sentences — fragments and single words ('Go.' 'Watch.' 'Pray.'); they need no explanation

character_relationships.to: Russian Empire / Tsar Nicholas I (off-stage opposite pole)

data.with: Russian Empire / Tsar Nicholas I (off-stage opposite pole)
data.type: civilizational antagonist — mirror image of imperial absolutism
data.quality: 30-year war; mutual incomprehension dressed as mutual contempt; structurally Shamil is to the Caucasus what Nicholas is to Russia
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Russia has resources, Shamil has terrain, faith, and Hadji Murad's now-vanished sword
data.speech_register_shift: When the Russians are mentioned: 'the infidel', 'the giaur', 'the dogs' — but never with vulgar heat, always with the dry register of a scholar describing vermin

character_relationships.to: His own family (wives in the harem, sons, especially the youngest)

data.with: His own family (wives in the harem, sons, especially the youngest)
data.type: the only relational sphere where the Imam dissolves into a man
data.quality: the single window onto vulnerability; cited by the novel as the place where Shamil shows fatigue, tenderness, even doubt
data.power_dynamic: patriarchal-absolute, but emotionally porous
data.speech_register_shift: Drops the third-person Imam form; speaks in first person; uses diminutives; allows pauses to be felt rather than calculated

data.quality: Genuine respect tinged with romantic orientalism; pledges protection in good faith and watches that pledge erode upward through the chain of command.
data.power_dynamic: Simon has formal power (rank, freedom, language); Hadji Murat has moral power (he is the man who trusted him). The dynamic inverts the longer they sit at the same table.
data.speech_register_shift: Drops French entirely with Hadji Murat. Slows his Russian, simplifies his syntax, uses Hadji Murat's name often. The voice becomes plainer, almost reverent, when they are alone.

data.quality: Affectionate, slightly indulgent; he relies on her as the household's emotional translator while he keeps his official mask on.
data.power_dynamic: Public deference to him, private equality; she sees the costs on his face that he will not put into words.
data.speech_register_shift: Most relaxed register; French phrases return; sentences shorten; the only person to whom he will admit, in fragments, that something is wrong.

data.quality: Filial respect intertwined with professional ambition and a quiet anxiety about adequacy.
data.power_dynamic: Heavily asymmetric upward — his father is both parent and commander-in-chief; almost everything Simon does is, in some sense, addressed to him.
data.speech_register_shift: Most formal of all; rank-titles ('Your Excellency', 'father' only in private letters); reports rather than converses.

data.quality: Courteous authority; runs his staff by example rather than fear; quietly disliked by careerists who find him too soft on the Avar.
data.power_dynamic: Clear hierarchy, gentled by manners.
data.speech_register_shift: Crisper, shorter sentences; military register; the warmth retracts but the courtesy does not.

data.quality: His tragedy lives here. He believes — and continues to believe — that the system he serves is honourable enough to honour his word for him. It is not.
data.power_dynamic: He is small. The institution is total.
data.speech_register_shift: Written-dispatch register: passive constructions, precedent-citations, no first person.

data.quality: Nicholas never meets Hadji Murat; he is the unseen hand whose policy seals his fate. To Nicholas, Hadji Murat is a file, a chess piece, a 'wild Asiatic' useful only as leverage against Shamil.
data.power_dynamic: absolute structural dominance — Nicholas signs, Hadji Murat dies
data.register_shift: speaks of him in the third person, by surname only, as one would name a hunting dog of uncertain temper

data.quality: tolerated instrument; Nicholas notes Chernyshov's flattery and uses him without affection
data.power_dynamic: Chernyshov stands, Nicholas sits — physical staging of the entire relationship
data.register_shift: clipped imperatives in Russian; never French, never warm

data.quality: respects competence, distrusts independence; reads his reports without thanking him
data.power_dynamic: delegated authority that can be revoked at any moment
data.register_shift: more formal, fewer interruptions; Nicholas listens — rare

data.quality: the masked-ball encounter (chunk_067-068) reveals appetite without intimacy; the Empress is decor, the masked woman is conquest
data.power_dynamic: imperial entitlement; consent is assumed
data.register_shift: French phrases, lowered voice, transactional charm — a different mask, same machinery

data.quality: two autocrats facing each other across the Caucasus, neither comprehending the other; for Nicholas, Shamil is fanaticism to be crushed; he never names him with respect
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Nicholas commands an empire, Shamil commands mountains; Nicholas does not see this as parity
data.register_shift: spoken of in policy terms only, never as a man

data.quality: complains they all steal, performs the role of the lone honest punisher; this is self-flattery, not analysis
data.power_dynamic: total
data.register_shift: moralising, weary, grandiose — the voice of a man performing virtue for an audience of one (himself)

data.quality: Reverent — Kazi-Mulla's death legitimates Hamzad's succession
data.power_dynamic: Inherits authority; speaks of him only to claim continuity
data.register_shift: Sacred-elegiac when invoked

data.quality: Trusted lieutenant who will inherit the Imamate after Hamzad's own assassination (historical, off-screen for HM's narrative)
data.power_dynamic: Hamzad above; Shamil below but rising
data.register_shift: Commanding, doctrinal — treats Shamil as instrument of the Ghazavat

data.type: Targeted enemy under the mask of negotiation
data.quality: Cold strategic courtesy followed by murder by proxy
data.power_dynamic: Hamzad seizes total power once her sons are dead; she is left alive only long enough to be silenced
data.register_shift: Honorific address ('mother of the Khans') used as cover; the kill order delivered wordlessly via a wink to Aseldar

data.quality: Lured into his tent under pretense of teaching the Ghazavat, then killed
data.power_dynamic: Inversion — guest-status weaponized; Hamzad violates the highland sacred bond of the host
data.register_shift: Warm hospitality formulas immediately before the order is given

data.quality: Received 'well' — performance of trust to extract the larger family
data.power_dynamic: Total — the child's life as collateral
data.register_shift: Paternal warmth used as bait

data.quality: Wordless trust — Hamzad winks, Aseldar strikes the Khansha down
data.power_dynamic: Master-instrument; violence delegated so Hamzad's own hand stays ritually clean
data.register_shift: No words at all — the relationship runs on gesture

data.quality: They never meet on-screen, but Hamzad's slaughter of HM's family (and the Khans HM served) is the foundational wound that drives HM's entire blood-feud arc and his eventual defection to the Russians against Shamil
data.power_dynamic: Posthumous — Hamzad shapes HM's life from the grave; HM's revenge logic is set in motion by Hamzad's act
data.register_shift: Hamzad is referenced in HM's recounted past and in a song HM hears the night before he dies (chunk_114) — Hamzad's name foreshadows HM's own end

data.type: Old friend, political patron and military superior
data.quality: Warm in private, deferential in form, mutually instrumentalizing in substance
data.power_dynamic: Vorontsov outranks him as Viceroy, but Baryatinsky carries the Tsar's direct mandate on the left flank — a quiet equilibrium
data.speech_register_shift: French phrases, first-name intimacy, longer sentences with rhetorical flourish

data.quality: Genuine attraction wrapped in social danger; never named publicly
data.power_dynamic: Asymmetric — his rank protects him, her reputation does not
data.speech_register_shift: Lowered voice, dropped formality, single-word sentences carrying weight

data.type: Strategic asset / colonial subject (no direct intimacy)
data.quality: Distant, instrumental — HM is a card to be played, not a man to be known
data.power_dynamic: Total in Baryatinsky's framing; he never grasps that HM has his own logic
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks about HM, almost never to him; uses third person and military designators

data.quality: Reverent, performative loyalty
data.power_dynamic: Absolute downward
data.speech_register_shift: Formal third-person reference ('His Majesty'), no irony, no qualification

data.quality: Cool, evaluating, occasionally cutting
data.power_dynamic: Total downward
data.speech_register_shift: Clipped imperatives, no French, no warmth

data.quality: impersonal at the level of conviction, charged with reluctant respect at the level of soldiering
data.power_dynamic: Karganov holds operational power (numbers, terrain, supply); Hadji Murat holds moral and martial stature — the inversion the scene exploits
data.speech_register_shift: shifts to the formal parley register — slower cadence, fixed formulas, no familiarity; refuses to dignify HM with his name in front of the troops

data.quality: correct, distant, demanding; loyalty rewarded with brevity, error punished with cold rebuke
data.power_dynamic: absolute within the district
data.speech_register_shift: rank-only address, imperative-heavy, no first names, no jokes

data.quality: deferential without warmth; sees them as the audience for his dispatches
data.power_dynamic: Karganov is an instrument of their will and knows it
data.speech_register_shift: formal written register; his speech tightens into report-language whenever they are invoked

character_relationships.to: Hadji Aga (Russian-aligned Avar commander, parley counterpart)

data.quality: uses Hadji Aga as cultural translator and front-line voice for the surrender call; respects his usefulness, distrusts his motives
data.power_dynamic: Karganov outranks but depends on him for legitimacy with the locals
data.speech_register_shift: shorter, more transactional; instructions framed as requests but unmistakably orders

data.quality: lethal, inherited, impersonal hatred dressed as duty
data.power_dynamic: subordinate while HM lives free; dominant the moment HM is unhorsed and exposed
data.speech_register_shift: addresses HM by full name only, never directly in dialogue if avoidable; in confrontation, drops to single-clause threat-register

data.quality: cold cooperation; mutual use without trust
data.power_dynamic: Karganov holds imperial authority; Arslan Khan holds the moral claim and the local knowledge
data.speech_register_shift: switches to broken, formal Russian; clipped military nouns; refuses any familiar register

data.quality: reverent, silent, non-negotiable
data.power_dynamic: the dead command him absolutely
data.speech_register_shift: softens only when naming them; uses possessives ('my father', 'my brother') as the warmest words he speaks in the script

data.quality: Severed bond carried as a wound he refuses to name; intimate knowledge of HM's tactics, body, dagger-style — used now as weapon
data.power_dynamic: On the battlefield: numerical superiority, Russian backing, parley-position. Morally: subordinate forever — HM is the one who chose, and HM is the one who dies standing.
data.speech_register_shift: Drops formal address ('Hadji Murad') to bare name in the assault; never uses the kunak-word; addresses HM directly only across distance, never within reach

data.quality: Functional, transactional, mutually distrustful; he is their instrument and knows it
data.power_dynamic: Subordinate in rank, indispensable in execution — the one man who can identify HM and lead the local levies
data.speech_register_shift: Strips honorifics, uses flat military Russian ('Polkovnik' at most, never 'Vashe Vysokoblagorodie'); shorter sentences, no Allah-invocations

data.quality: Authority by lineage and reputation; followers obey but do not love
data.power_dynamic: Absolute on the field, brittle off it — he must keep delivering victories or the levy melts
data.speech_register_shift: Imperative-only, no first-person plural; commands by name when possible to bind individual loyalty

data.type: Replaced — Hadji Aga inherits the parley-voice beat
data.quality: N/A — function consolidated
data.power_dynamic: N/A
data.speech_register_shift: N/A

character_relationships.to: Akhmet Khan (absorbed via fusion — see fusion_decision)

data.type: Replaced — Hadji Aga inherits the 'Caucasian-ally-of-Russia' antagonist function
data.quality: N/A — function consolidated; any informant/lineage detail folded into Hadji Aga's backstory or a single dagger-line
data.power_dynamic: N/A
data.speech_register_shift: N/A

data.type: petitioner / unhelpful patron (historical, off-screen for most of film)
data.quality: cold, transactional, ultimately dismissive
data.power_dynamic: Rosen holds bureaucratic power; HM holds moral and martial authority Rosen cannot acknowledge
data.register_shift: addresses HM through interpreter, by title only, never closing the social distance

data.quality: indifferent
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Rosen is gatekeeper
data.register_shift: identical formal register, no individuation

data.type: predecessor / successor relationship in the Caucasus command (Vorontsov supersedes Rosen's failed approach)
data.quality: implicit institutional contrast — Vorontsov engages where Rosen withheld
data.power_dynamic: Rosen is the past, Vorontsov the present
data.register_shift: n/a (no direct scene)

data.quality: absolute submission
data.power_dynamic: Hamzad dominant; Aseldar is instrument
data.register_shift: lowers eyes, reduces speech to confirmations and reports

data.quality: rivalrous loyalty under same imam, then mortal enemy after the Khansha killing
data.power_dynamic: parity in skill, opposed in ethic
data.register_shift: cold formal address; no fraternal warmth even in shared cause

data.quality: no relationship — she is an object of the deed
data.power_dynamic: total asymmetry
data.register_shift: does not address her at all; speaks the formula, then strikes

data.quality: Distant, formal, transactional respect across an unbridgeable cultural line
data.power_dynamic: Rosen high in the past tense; in present tense, a faded credential Hadji Murad invokes
data.speech_register_shift: Switches to slow, ceremonial Russian — the register of granting rank — and avoids first-person warmth

data.type: Successor in command of the Caucasus
data.quality: Professional courtesy masking a generational handover Rosen resents
data.power_dynamic: Vorontsov institutionally ascendant; Rosen's authority survives only as precedent
data.speech_register_shift: Becomes more formally correct, almost stilted, addressing Vorontsov by full title

data.quality: Loyal subordinate of an autocrat he has never personally questioned
data.power_dynamic: Total deference
data.speech_register_shift: Court register; eyes lowered in reported speech; no opinion offered unless asked

data.quality: Adversary respected as a problem of state, not as a man
data.power_dynamic: Mutually unyielding; neither has met the other
data.speech_register_shift: Names him only as 'the Imam' — refusing the personal name as a small administrative violence

data.type: Master / sworn murid; surrogate father-son bond layered over military discipleship
data.quality: Absolute, wordless devotion; the central relationship of Eldar's life and the lens through which he reads every other person.
data.power_dynamic: Total subordination — HM commands by glance; Eldar obeys before the sentence ends. Eldar's only sphere of independent judgement is the threat assessment of strangers.
data.speech_register_shift: Lowest, softest, most clipped register; eyes down, voice barely above the room. Religious vocatives ('Agha') used naturally.

data.quality: Wordless solidarity; they divide the watch without negotiation. No rivalry, no warmth — a working trust between two silent men.
data.power_dynamic: Roughly equal among the murids; Eldar slightly senior in HM's intimate orbit.
data.speech_register_shift: Even more compressed than with HM — often pure gesture; when he speaks, it is operational ('You take the door.').

data.type: Brother-murid, but a different temperament — Khan Mahoma talks, Eldar does not
data.quality: Quiet tolerance; Eldar registers Khan Mahoma's volubility as a small risk to be managed near HM.
data.power_dynamic: Equal in rank; Eldar's stillness exerts a subtle disciplinary pressure on the others.
data.speech_register_shift: Curt, almost monosyllabic; refuses to be drawn into banter.

data.type: Brother-murid, senior in years and harshness
data.quality: Respect without affection; Eldar accepts Gamzalo's grimness as a model of the discipline he himself embodies more softly.
data.power_dynamic: Gamzalo slightly above by age and severity; Eldar defers in logistics, never in the matter of HM's personal safety.
data.speech_register_shift: Formal, brief, deferential where pragmatic; immovable when HM is the subject.

data.type: Hosts / captors / strangers across a cultural and political line
data.quality: Wary courtesy; he treats them as potential threats in fine clothing and never relaxes his body in their presence.
data.power_dynamic: Nominally below them inside the fort; in his own moral hierarchy they are guests in HM's orbit, not the other way round.
data.speech_register_shift: Almost mute; answers only direct questions, in the shortest grammatically complete form, never volunteering.

character_relationships.to: Maria Dmitrievna and the women of the fort

data.type: Outsiders to the murid bond; alien gender register
data.quality: Respectful distance; he lowers his eyes further, will not be alone with them, treats kindness as a complication rather than a gift.
data.power_dynamic: Asymmetric and culturally coded — he gives way physically but does not adopt their idiom.
data.speech_register_shift: Reduced almost to gesture; a small bow of the head replaces most sentences.

data.type: host / fascinated witness — Russian officer to Avar naib
data.quality: respectful, slightly awed, quietly envious of HM's wholeness; the relationship is the moral mirror of the screenplay
data.power_dynamic: officially Butler is the host and HM the guest-prisoner; emotionally HM is the senior man, and Butler knows it
data.register_shift: drops the bright Petersburg manner; sentences shorten, become more formal and more sincere; uses learned Tartar greetings

data.type: billet-comrade and friend — fellow line officer, married to Marya Dmitrievna
data.quality: warm, loyal, faintly guilty (because of Marya Dmitrievna); the relationship anchors Butler's restraint
data.power_dynamic: rough equality of rank; Petrov is the steadier, plainer man, Butler the more polished
data.register_shift: easy, fraternal, full of 'my dear fellow' and shared silences; obscenity still avoided

data.type: comrade's mistress / unspoken object of attraction
data.quality: tender, courteous, deliberately fraternal; Butler maintains 'a simple, respectful tone' precisely because he feels otherwise
data.power_dynamic: she has the moral authority of the household; Butler defers
data.register_shift: exaggerated courtesy, French politesse, careful eye-contact — the manners covering desire

data.type: subordinate / junior officer to garrison superior
data.quality: outwardly correct, inwardly contemptuous of their drunkenness and brutality, though he shares both
data.power_dynamic: clearly subordinate; obeys, salutes, drinks when ordered
data.register_shift: stiffer, more formulaic, regimental phrases, no irony allowed to surface

data.quality: buoyant, anecdotal, performatively cheerful — the public face of the campaign
data.power_dynamic: rough equality; Butler is slightly senior in style by virtue of his Guards background
data.register_shift: bright, quick, full of campaign slang and song-quotations

character_relationships.to: the Chechens / villagers of the burned aoul

data.quality: almost no direct speech to them; the relationship is one of guilty witness rather than dialogue
data.power_dynamic: absolute military dominance, moral inversion
data.register_shift: silence, or curt orders relayed through NCOs; the brightness drains from his voice

data.quality: affectionate, slightly maternal, conspiratorial; she is his unacknowledged co-administrator of Tiflis society
data.power_dynamic: publicly his consort, privately his equal — she edits him in French while he speaks Russian
data.register_shift: in private: faster, more familiar, French diminutives, dry edits of his evening; in public: ceremonious, deferential, finishes his half-sentences only when they begin to falter

data.type: hostess and exotic guest; an unexpected, unspoken fascination
data.quality: warm, curious, slightly disturbed; he is the first guest she cannot fully translate
data.power_dynamic: she is the social superior in her own house and yet, in his presence, briefly the supplicant — the language barrier inverts her usual advantage
data.register_shift: speaks more slowly, drops the irony, prefers French even though he understands neither French nor Russian; the lightness becomes a kind of formality

data.quality: indulgent, flattering, faintly bored
data.power_dynamic: absolute within the drawing-room; she decides who is interesting this season
data.register_shift: fluid French-Russian code-switching, abundant endearments, tag questions that disguise commands as invitations

data.type: (post-fusion) the same role — she now also delivers the single intervention that interrupts a general's tactless remark at the Tiflis dinner
data.quality: social-correction by elegance, never by reproach
data.power_dynamic: her interruption is unanswerable because it is phrased as concern for the guest's comfort
data.register_shift: from drawing-room banter to one short, perfectly weighted French sentence that closes the subject

data.quality: Loyal, exasperated, maternal; built on shared loneliness rather than romance
data.power_dynamic: He has the rank and the legal standing; she has the daily authority — over the food, the drink, the household, and, quietly, over his dignity
data.speech_register_shift: Drops to the most informal register, mixes scolding with endearments ('batyushka', 'you fool'); uses imperatives freely

data.type: Hostess to a guest who is also a political prisoner of sorts
data.quality: Respectful, curious, increasingly sympathetic; she treats him as a man before she treats him as an enemy
data.power_dynamic: Formally she is the Russian woman of the house and he the surrendered chieftain, but morally she comes to feel he is the more upright of the two
data.speech_register_shift: More careful, slightly formal; fewer scolding tics; uses 'batyushka' with real respect rather than irony

data.type: Reluctant hostess to a foreign retainer
data.quality: Friction — cultural and domestic; they quarrel over the running of the household
data.power_dynamic: She runs the house, he answers only to Hadji Murad; neither will fully yield
data.speech_register_shift: Sharper, more clipped; orders rather than requests; no endearments

data.type: Companion / informal protector after Petrov's decline
data.quality: Affectionate but disillusioned; he is the witness to her final moral outburst
data.power_dynamic: She is older in experience of the frontier; he in rank — in practice they meet as equals in disenchantment
data.speech_register_shift: Plain, confiding, occasionally tender; she allows herself the rare 'you know' and the rare admission

character_relationships.to: Russian officers parading Hadji Murad's head

data.quality: Open contempt; they are the men her loyalty was supposed to extend to and no longer can
data.power_dynamic: They have the trophy, the rank, and the drink; she has only her voice — and uses it
data.speech_register_shift: Flat, hard, no endearments, no religious softening; the only time in the story she calls Russian soldiers what she thinks they are

data.quality: Loyal, professional, mutually respectful; Vorontsov genuinely trusts him with the most delicate Caucasian dossier.
data.power_dynamic: Strongly asymmetric — Vorontsov holds rank, class, and ear of the Tsar; Loris-Melikov holds linguistic and cultural access Vorontsov cannot replace.
data.speech_register_shift: Most formal Russian register; full titles, conditional constructions, never first-name. Voice quiets; sentences shorten.

data.type: Official handler / recorder of his life story; de facto confidant
data.quality: Professionally correct, privately respectful; the only Russian-side figure Hadji Murat speaks to without an interpreter between them.
data.power_dynamic: Officially Loris-Melikov controls access, schedule, and how HM's words reach the Russians; in private the moral weight tilts to Hadji Murat, and Loris-Melikov knows it.
data.speech_register_shift: Switches into Tatar; rhythm slows, formality drops a degree, occasional 'brother'; questions become open rather than directive.

data.type: Aide to the Prince's household; social facilitator
data.quality: Courtly, deferential, useful to her in arranging Hadji Murat's presentation at receptions.
data.power_dynamic: She outranks him socially; he is the discreet functionary who makes her salons work.
data.speech_register_shift: Court-French politeness layered on Russian; lighter, more decorative phrasing; never mentions intelligence work in her presence.

data.quality: Cool, correct; they suspect his Caucasian sympathies, he suspects their dispatches misrepresent the field.
data.power_dynamic: They have institutional weight; he has Vorontsov's confidence and the actual transcripts.
data.speech_register_shift: Stiffened, maximally formal; declarative sentences, no Tatar idioms allowed to surface, even in passing.

data.type: Functional duplicate within the same scene-space (Tatar-speaking trusted intermediary)
data.quality: Redundant — same dramatic role, fewer scenes; in this adaptation his narrative function is absorbed by Loris-Melikov.
data.power_dynamic: N/A in feature: Tarkhanov collapses into Loris-Melikov per fusion logic.
data.speech_register_shift: N/A — Tarkhanov is fused away; Loris-Melikov inherits any scene-essential beat.

data.type: Sworn blood-brother and murid; nursed by HM's mother Patimat in the ritual that ended the feud between their families
data.quality: Reverential, total, almost filial — HM is master, brother, and saviour in one
data.power_dynamic: Khanefi absolutely subordinate; obeys without comment
data.speech_register_shift: Voice drops, addresses HM as 'agha'; sentences shorten; eyes lower

data.type: Fellow murid; closest peer in HM's inner circle
data.quality: Wordless solidarity; the two move as a single body around HM
data.power_dynamic: Equal, but Eldar is the active companion and Khanefi the household steward
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks Avar in clipped half-sentences; often communicates by gesture alone

data.type: Fellow murid; the warrior beside the steward
data.quality: Cool respect; each guards a different flank of HM's life
data.power_dynamic: Parallel functions; neither commands the other
data.speech_register_shift: Operational, terse — orders about horses, watches, ammunition

data.quality: Quiet disapproval of Khan Mahoma's frivolity; tolerates him for HM's sake
data.power_dynamic: Equal in rank; Khanefi the gravity to Khan Mahoma's lightness
data.speech_register_shift: Drier, shorter, refuses banter

data.quality: Mentoring without warmth — corrects, does not praise
data.power_dynamic: Senior; gives instructions about pack-horses and weapons
data.speech_register_shift: Imperative mode, shorter sentences

data.type: Russian woman at the fort who expelled him from her kitchen
data.quality: Combustible, near-violent — the only relationship in which his rage shows openly
data.power_dynamic: Khanefi denied agency in her domain, responds with the dagger he never draws on his own
data.speech_register_shift: Broken, percussive Russian; or no words, only the threat in his body

data.type: Foster-mother through the milk ritual; the woman who ended the blood feud by nursing him
data.quality: Sacred; the source of every loyalty he has
data.power_dynamic: Filial reverence — she is the reason he lives
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks her name only in invocation, never in narration

data.quality: devoted, playful, slightly worshipful; the youngest-feeling of the four murids
data.power_dynamic: fully subordinate; volunteers for the most exposed errands
data.speech_register_shift: drops the joking opener; voice tightens to clipped report-Russian/Avar; calls him only 'Hadji Murad', never a diminutive

data.quality: affectionate friction — the joker against the earnest one
data.power_dynamic: equal in rank, opposite in temperament
data.speech_register_shift: louder, more teasing; uses Khanefi as straight man for jokes about ritual and prayer

data.quality: protective, mocking-fond
data.power_dynamic: Khan Mahoma the worldlier, Eldar the more silent — Khan Mahoma fills Eldar's silences
data.speech_register_shift: softer, almost gentle; teases without sting

data.type: operational partner on the Vorontsov mission
data.quality: wary cooperation; insider murid vs. hired Chechen guide
data.power_dynamic: equal in the field but Khan Mahoma carries Hadji Murad's words and therefore the authority
data.speech_register_shift: terser, more transactional; fewer jokes, no diminutives

data.quality: performed deference masking sharp observation
data.power_dynamic: asymmetric — Khan Mahoma is the supplicant's mouth
data.speech_register_shift: broken Russian deliberately exaggerated; bows in word and posture; jokes withheld until safely outside the gates

data.quality: feared and unspoken
data.power_dynamic: Khan Mahoma's life and his master's family hang on Shamil's mood
data.speech_register_shift: name lowered to a near-whisper; jokes vanish entirely when Shamil is mentioned

data.type: Unconfessed romantic infatuation across a social barrier (she is married, her husband is his superior's circle)
data.quality: Tender, distracted, slightly absurd — observed by everyone except, he hopes, her
data.power_dynamic: She holds all of it; his rank cannot reach her station as hostess and married woman
data.speech_register_shift: Drops into his most polished, French-flecked, ironic-gallant register; sentences shorten and slow; never directly addresses feeling

data.type: Old comrade — fellow Cadet College alumnus, now both posted in the Caucasus
data.quality: Easy, fraternal, the shorthand of shared schooldays
data.power_dynamic: Roughly equal; mutual ribbing without status anxiety
data.speech_register_shift: Most relaxed register — schoolboy nicknames, half-finished sentences, mess-room laughter

data.quality: Paternal-cheerful in the Guards manner — courteous to NCOs, formally correct, not intimate
data.power_dynamic: Absolute authority, lightly worn
data.speech_register_shift: Drops the French and the irony; voice becomes clipped, declarative, audible at distance

data.type: Enemy commander turned object of fascinated Russian observation
data.quality: Curiosity tinged with the romance of the 'noble mountaineer' — Poltoratsky reads him through Petersburg eyes
data.power_dynamic: None directly; Poltoratsky is a witness, not an interlocutor
data.speech_register_shift: Speaks of him in the elevated, slightly theatrical register reserved for legends — the Caucasus made into a story he can tell back home

data.type: Subordinate to a circle he socially belongs to but militarily depends on
data.quality: Punctilious deference layered over salon familiarity
data.power_dynamic: They outrank him; he flatters without grovelling
data.speech_register_shift: Formal address, military correctness, the French tags retained as a class-marker rather than a flourish

data.quality: Total, unspoken loyalty; pre-verbal trust
data.power_dynamic: Subordinate by oath, equal in the moment of combat
data.register_shift: Speaks least of all, addresses him as 'Hadji Agha', never contradicts in front of others; in private will state a tactical disagreement in one flat sentence and then obey.

data.type: Fellow murid, comrade in HM's inner circle
data.quality: Functional brotherhood — household-keeper and warrior, two halves of one service
data.power_dynamic: Roughly equal; Gamzalo carries the harder edge
data.register_shift: Curt, practical, two-word exchanges about horses, watches, supplies; no warmth in language, full warmth in reliance.

data.quality: Quiet mentorship under fire; corrects by example, not lecture
data.power_dynamic: Gamzalo leads, Eldar follows
data.register_shift: Imperatives only — 'Take the left', 'Eyes up' — never explanations.

data.type: Hosts who are also captors in his eyes
data.quality: Cold civility on the surface; absolute distrust beneath
data.power_dynamic: Outwardly subordinate guest, inwardly an armed witness
data.register_shift: Speaks only when addressed, in the shortest possible answers, never volunteers; declines Russian forms of politeness.

data.quality: Reverence for the office, no comment on the man's quarrel with Hadji Murad
data.power_dynamic: Absent authority; Gamzalo refuses to weigh in
data.register_shift: Will not name Shamil in conversation if he can avoid it; if forced, uses the title only.

data.quality: Respect-based, professional loyalty that hardens into personal loyalty; chosen, not inherited
data.power_dynamic: Hadji Murad commands; Bata serves — but Bata's value lies in skills the leader cannot replace, giving him a quiet leverage
data.speech_register_shift: Drops boasting almost entirely with HM; speaks shorter, more direct, defers without grovelling — the only man around whom he stops performing

data.type: Mission partner; insider-murid paired with outsider-guide
data.quality: Functional alliance with quiet rivalry — they need each other but represent two different ways of belonging to Hadji Murad
data.power_dynamic: Roughly equal in mission terms; Khan Mahoma has rank-by-name, Bata has skill-by-terrain
data.speech_register_shift: Banter in Chechen/Avar, mountain humor, clipped jokes about who is leading whom; switches to formal cooperation when Russians are present

data.quality: Older-brother-on-the-road dynamic; Bata's authority is teacher-like without tenderness
data.power_dynamic: Bata leads, Eldar follows — Bata's experience outranks Eldar's loyalty
data.speech_register_shift: Instructional, terse, occasional dry humor; uses imperative more than with anyone else

data.quality: Wary, transactional; Bata performs harmlessness deliberately
data.power_dynamic: They hold the rifles; he holds the message — temporary parity through usefulness
data.speech_register_shift: Broken Russian, simplified syntax, repeats key words, drops all proverbs, exaggerates politeness ('friend', 'sir'); body language goes deliberately small

data.type: Fellow murid in the final stand
data.quality: Comrade-in-arms by circumstance; functional bond at the end
data.power_dynamic: Equal — both subordinate to Hadji Murad's last decisions
data.speech_register_shift: Almost wordless; the talkative guide goes quiet at the end, communicating in glances and small actions

data.quality: Asymmetric, sycophantic, transactional — Chernyshov serves Nicholas's vanity in exchange for protection and standing.
data.power_dynamic: Absolute imbalance: Nicholas dictates, Chernyshov decodes and confirms.
data.speech_register_shift: Maximum deference; French ornamentation, third-person address ('your Imperial Majesty'); never initiates, only responds; tone modulates to mirror the Tsar's humor.

data.type: Bureaucratic rival; co-minister of imperial machinery
data.quality: Cold professional courtesy in writing (French letters), private resentment and undermining at court — envies Vorontsov's stature in the Caucasus and the respect he commands.
data.power_dynamic: Roughly equal in formal rank, but Chernyshov holds the proximity-to-Tsar advantage and weaponizes it.
data.speech_register_shift: In correspondence: elaborate court French, elaborate respect ('mon cher Prince'); when reporting on him to Nicholas: even-toned, factual, with strategically chosen omissions and emphases.

data.type: Junior ministerial colleague / portfolio assistant in the Nicholas-cabinet scenes
data.quality: Functional, hierarchical; Dolgoruky carries papers, Chernyshov carries the agenda.
data.power_dynamic: Chernyshov is senior; Dolgoruky defers.
data.speech_register_shift: Brisk, instructive, slightly impatient — the only register in which Chernyshov permits himself mild authority.

data.type: Subject of dispatches; never met in person
data.quality: Abstract — a name on a report, a problem to be administered; no human dimension granted.
data.power_dynamic: Total bureaucratic distance; Chernyshov holds the pen, Hadji Murat is ink.
data.speech_register_shift: Reduces him to administrative noun-phrases ('the Avar', 'the deserter', 'the prisoner under guard at Nukha').

data.quality: Deep, unstated loyalty masked as gruff teasing; Panov is the witness to Avdeev's quiet sacrifice and his death
data.power_dynamic: Panov is older, NCO, the steady one; Avdeev is the soft-hearted private. Panov leads, but Avdeev's death exposes that the moral weight sits with the private, not the NCO.
data.register_shift: Drops from camp-banter and orders into near-whispered diminutives (Avdeyushka) and one-word reassurances at the deathbed.

data.quality: Respectful, professional; Panov is the experienced enlisted anchor who quietly informs the young officer about the field — including identifying Shamil's banner.
data.power_dynamic: Officer/NCO formal hierarchy; in practice Panov knows the ground better and Butler knows it.
data.register_shift: Tightens to clipped, deferential 'your honour' register; shorter sentences, no proverbs, pipe out of mouth.

character_relationships.to: Soldiers under his command (Petrukha, Bondarenko, Nikitin, Serogin in the source roster)

data.quality: Paternal-fraternal; mediates their squabbles, indulges their jokes, draws the line at carelessness on watch.
data.power_dynamic: Quiet authority by experience rather than rank-fetish; obeyed because he is right, not because he shouts.
data.register_shift: Uses diminutives and proverbs freely; teases; sharpens to flat imperatives only when discipline or safety demands it.

data.quality: Wary professional curiosity; Panov reads Hadji Murat the way he reads weather — as a fact of the field, not a moral question.
data.power_dynamic: None direct; Panov is line-soldier, HM is the legend at the officers' table.
data.register_shift: Speaks of him only in third person, dryly, to other enlisted men; never to his face.

data.quality: Warm, unequal — Panov calls him 'smart chap'; Avdeev accepts the nickname with quiet pleasure
data.power_dynamic: Panov senior in experience; Avdeev defers easily, without resentment
data.speech_register_shift: Slightly more clipped and soldierly around Panov; fewer half-sentences, more 'yes, brother'

data.quality: Easy, joking, mutually protective; the only social world he has in the Caucasus
data.power_dynamic: Equal; he is the cheerful one others lean on for small repairs and small laughs
data.speech_register_shift: Loosest register here — village idioms, jokes, complaints about food and weather

data.type: Family — the brother whose lot he took
data.quality: Loved, missed, faintly resented in a way he will not name
data.power_dynamic: Avdeev gave up his life for the brother's; the debt runs in both directions and neither speaks of it
data.speech_register_shift: Softer, slower, more rural — when he speaks of him, the soldier-slang drops away

data.type: Family — distant, barely known after years of service
data.quality: Tender, abstract, almost imagined; she exists for him as a direction more than a person
data.power_dynamic: Absent; the relationship survives on letters and memory
data.speech_register_shift: Quietest register — short sentences, no jokes, often a question he does not finish

data.quality: Polite, opaque; he performs the cheerful soldier they want to see
data.power_dynamic: Total — they name him only when he dies, and then only as a statistic
data.speech_register_shift: Stiff, clipped, all 'yes sir' and 'no sir'; the real Avdeev disappears entirely

data.type: Structural — the system that owns his life
data.quality: Unseen, unanswerable; he never addresses it directly
data.power_dynamic: He is a unit; it is a machine — the gap between 'Avdeev' and 'one private killed' is the whole point of his arc
data.speech_register_shift: He never speaks to it; when it is named near him, he goes quiet

data.type: Father–son; the relational engine of HM's entire defection arc
data.quality: Worshipful, wounded, unfinished — Yusuf adores a father he has barely had time to be known by, and that incompleteness is the wound the story turns on.
data.power_dynamic: Total asymmetry: HM is the actor in the world above; Yusuf is the stake in the pit below. Yusuf has only one power — to be loved enough to be saved.
data.speech_register_shift: With or about his father, Yusuf shifts into a slower, more formal Avar, borrowing his father's measured cadences; this is the only register in which his sentences reliably finish.

data.type: Captor / sovereign judge; the man who holds his life in his hand
data.quality: Fear masked as defiance; Yusuf knows Shamil is not a monster, which makes the verdict harder to bear.
data.power_dynamic: Absolute — Shamil decides whether he lives, dies, or is mutilated as a sign.
data.speech_register_shift: In Shamil's presence (or imagined presence) Yusuf's voice tightens into formal, pious phrasing; behind his back it collapses into curses and bravado.

data.type: Mother; the private self he protects from his public bravery
data.quality: Tender, almost unspoken; she is the name he will not say in front of guards.
data.power_dynamic: Emotional — she has no power to free him, but her grief is the one judgment he cannot bear.
data.speech_register_shift: Softer, simpler diction; sentences shorten and trail off; the only register in which he comes close to admitting fear.

data.type: Captors of his own people; ambivalent peers
data.quality: Contemptuous on the surface, kin underneath — they are Chechens and Avars like him, doing a job he might one day have done.
data.power_dynamic: Day-to-day power over food, light, movement; long-term they are interchangeable instruments of Shamil's will.
data.speech_register_shift: Sharper, more dialectal, full of insults and proverbs; uses shared idiom to remind them — and himself — that he is one of them, not a Russian's son.

data.type: Distant power his father has run to; both possible saviors and historic enemies
data.quality: Suspicion braided with hope; he hates needing them.
data.power_dynamic: Abstract — he never meets them on the page, but his fate is being negotiated in their language.
