plot_elements.description: Author-Narrator gathers wildflowers in a midsummer field during harvest season, observing the variety and beauty of blooms

Author-Narrator's reflection on human destructiveness and the thistle's indomitable vitality — 'Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit.' — pivots into recall of a Caucasian episode.
Narrative frame pivot: Author-Narrator introduces the Caucasian episode as partly witnessed, partly heard, partly imagined — setting November 1851 as temporal anchor for the main story.
Hadji Murad stops at a specific saklya, wakes the old man with a whip-handle tap, and is recognized — implies prior relationship and a network of safe houses.
The woman of the house receives Hadji Murad with the traditional hospitality greeting and begins preparing cushions — Chechen guest-right (kunak culture) is activated.
Eldar introduced as Hadji Murad's murid — mirrors leader's disarming ritual, seated beside him; establishes the murid group as a unit
Hadji Murad decides to send a letter to the Russians via his murid Eldar; Sado volunteers his brother Bata as guide — the defection-to-Russians is set in motion.

Hadji Murad retrieves a secret note from his cartridge-pouch and entrusts it to Sado to forward to his son — first explicit signal that he is working to communicate with his family trapped under Shamil

Chapter II begins: POV shifts to Vozvizhensk fort; Panov leads three Russian soldiers to a night ambush position at the blackened plane tree — establishes the Russian military presence surrounding the same geography Hadji Murad occupies.
Soldiers gossip about company commander dipping into the cashbox after card losses; Panov defers to collective company decision
Company commander has borrowed from the communal chest again; soldiers debate whether to demand an accounting — Nikitin insists, Panov and Avdeev counsel patience.
Interior of Vorontsov's house at midnight: lavish card game while all other barracks are dark — the imperial outpost lives in two worlds simultaneously
Marya Vasilevna's proximity reduces Poltoratsky to distraction, costing him aces and angering the adjutant — flirtation as social currency in the fort
Poltoratsky finds his door locked; scolds the drunk Vovilo, stops short of striking him
Vovilo's interior monologue: compares his lot to Ivan Petrovich's, despairs over his serf condition and the hollow promise of freedom post-Caucasus
Poltoratsky retires to bed, daydreaming about Marya Vasilevna; orders Vovilo to wake him at five for the morning expedition
Hadji Murad decides immediately to flee; Sado confirms horses are already saddled — showing Sado anticipated the danger.
Hadji Murad daydreams/fantasizes about defeating Shamil with Russian army support, ruling all of Chechnya — reveals his hidden ambition and transactional view of defection

Two Russian companies under Poltoratsky are felling trees beyond the Shagirinsk Gate in the Shalin glade; officers (Poltoratsky, Tikhonov, Baron Freze and two others) eat, drink vodka and porter on drums while awaiting events
Russian officers drink vodka and porter on drums after an operation, discussing the romanticised death of General Sleptsov; ironic gap between war mythology and reality is explicit
Poltoratsky mounts and leads his company toward the incoming fire, relishing the prospect of combat
Poltoratsky mounts and leads his company toward firing on the ravine outskirts; Chechen pursuers of Hadji Murad exchange shots with the Russian line.
Vorontsov reflects on jurisdictional tension with General Meller-Zakomelsky — he bypassed the chain of command and fears repercussions even amid triumph
Alone in his room, Hadji Murad drops his public mask: anxiety replaces courtesy. He checks on murids' arms, horses, and accommodations via Eldar.
Vorontsov's watch chimes; Marya Vasilevna prompts her husband to give it as a gift; Hadji Murad accepts with visible delight — a diplomatic gesture that softens the surface tension.
Marya Vasilevna insists on accompanying the men to Meller-Zakomelsky despite her husband's objections, asserting personal agency inside Russian military politics.
Simultaneous with Avdeev's death, his family threshes oats on the farm — domestic life indifferent to his sacrifice
Aksinya, Peter's widow, also learns of his death and laments, mourning both husband and her own ruined life.
Aksinya performs public grief for Peter Avdeev while privately rejoicing — his death frees her to marry the shopman who fathered her second child. Hypocrisy under social pressure is laid bare.
Vorontsov dismisses the jurisdictional quarrel between Simon and Meller-Zakomelsky — 'Simon a eu tort. But all's well that ends well'
Vorontsov reads Simon's letter about Hadji Murad's surrender and Simon's clash with Meller-Zakomelsky; dismisses Simon's fault with characteristic patrician ease ('All's well that ends well').
Hadji Murad reveals his family are hostages of Shamil — he will not openly act against Shamil until they are exchanged for Russian prisoners; this is the central constraint on his cooperation
Chapter XI opens: on the fifth day Loris-Melikov arrives by Vorontsov's command to formally record Hadji Murad's life story for transmission to the Tsar — institutional memoir-extraction begins.
Hadji Murad begins his oral autobiography: birth in Tselmess, family's closeness to the Khunzakh Khans through wet-nursing.

Hadji Murad's detailed childhood memory: the fountain, head-shaving, the brass mirror, his grandfather's silverwork, his mother's lepeshki. These form his psychological baseline — what he is fighting to recover.
Hadji Murad recalls palace life with the three Khans (Abu Nutsal, Umma, Bulach), his position of privilege, and wealth — the world before Muridism disrupted everything.
The arrival of Murid missionaries ('beat stones with wooden scimitars, cried Ghazavat') triggers mass conversion of Chechens and Avars — the historical tide that will sweep Hadji Murad into the war.

Kazi-Mulla is killed, Hamzad succeeds him, and Hamzad sends envoys demanding the Khans join the Ghazavat — the political pressure that leads directly to the assassinations Hadji Murad will narrate next.
Hadji Murad recounts his early prosperity and pleasure-seeking life before the Ghazavat
Hamzad threatens the Khans: join the Ghazavat or face destruction of Khunzakh
Baron Rosen refuses audience; Russian officers corrupt Umma Khan with wine, women, and card games — he loses everything
Khansha sends Bulach Khan as hostage to Hamzad; Hamzad invites the two elder brothers to follow
Hamzad sends a sheik offering Ghazavat instruction in exchange for the Khansha's youngest son as hostage — a calculated first step to lure all three Avar Khans.
Shots fired in Hamzad's tent — the massacre of the Avar Khans begins (off-screen within this chunk, mid-sentence cut).
Khan Mahoma smokes secretly in defiance of Hadji Murad's prohibition; Gamzalo rebukes him and leaves — establishing Gamzalo as the strict loyalist and Khan Mahoma as the irreverent pragmatist.

Loris-Melikov suspects Hadji Murad's surrender may be a spy mission — Gamzalo's open hatred of Russians reinforces the suspicion. Russian intelligence anxiety about HM's true motives surfaces explicitly.
HM summons Loris-Melikov back to the drawing room to resume narrating his life story (Chapter XIII opens). Storytelling-as-diplomacy thread continues.
Hadji Murad continues dictating his life story to Loris-Melikov in the drawing room — the flashback narrative frame resumes
Hamzad murders the Khansha on a murid's sword after taking the palace — completing the annihilation of the Avar Khan family
Hadji Murad and his brother Osman devise a blood-revenge ambush against Hamzad after consulting their grandfather; the plot is overheard and Hamzad warns the grandfather
Mosque assassination: Hadji Murad kills Aseldar with a dagger, Osman shoots Hamzad, Hadji Murad delivers the killing blow — Osman dies in the ensuing fight while Hadji Murad fights off thirty murids
Hadji Murad regains leadership over the Avars after recovering at Tselmess — confirms his political restoration and the power vacuum he filled.
Hadji Murad narrates his many military exploits to Loris-Melikov — a retrospective summary of campaigns already partly known to Loris-Melikov.
Hadji Murad concludes his life story to Loris-Melikov: Shamil's broken promise, the dismissal from Naib, seizure of property, and forced defection to Vorontsov — family still held hostage by Shamil
Hadji Murad explicitly states his constraint: 'I am tied up and the end of the rope is in Shamil's hands' — family hostage situation named as the central dramatic obstacle
Concern that Shamil will spread a rumor that Russians are imprisoning Hadji Murat — image management as political weapon in both directions

Vorontsov's letter concludes: Tarkhanov confirms Hadji Murad's sincerity and his certainty that Shamil will execute him if he returns — the only anxiety is HM's religious attachment, which Shamil might exploit.

Chernyshov attempts to use his New Year's Day report to Nicholas to portray Vorontsov's handling of Hadji Murad as naive and dangerous — proposing HM be transferred to Central Russia as a spy suspect.
Chernyshov's plan fails because Nicholas is in ill temper on New Year's Day and refuses suggestions from anyone, especially Chernyshov, whom he privately despises for the Decembrist betrayal.
Nicholas's knowledge of Chernyshov's treachery against Zachary Chernyshov (Decembrist trial) is revealed — ironic moral hypocrisy at the imperial court: Tsar keeps a blackguard as minister.
Chernyshov arrives at the Winter Palace in elaborate dress — visual tableau of imperial bureaucratic ritual, cold Petersburg morning.

Chernyshov arrives at the Winter Palace in full uniform regalia, performing the elaborate ritual of imperial court entry — cloak, wig, mirror, epaulets adjusted — before ascending to Nicholas's cabinet
The aide-de-camp fetches Chernyshov and Dolgoruky into Nicholas's cabinet — the physical crossing of the threshold into imperial power is rendered with exaggerated deference
Nicholas I is fatigued and irate after a masquerade the night before, where his 'senile sensuality' was aroused by a masked woman he pursued and finally isolated in a private box
Nicholas tracks and finally corners the masquerade woman in a private box, only to find it occupied by an Uhlan officer and his companion
Nicholas lies awake on his hard narrow bed under his famous cloak, comparing the girl to Nelidova — no moral reflection whatsoever, illustrating his total moral opacity
Nicholas justifies last night's seduction of a young girl by suppressing guilt with thoughts of his own greatness — establishes his moral self-insulation as a structural character trait.

Nicholas spots Helena Pavlovna's carriage approaching the palace; her presence triggers contempt and class-based hostility in him — he sees her as emblem of liberal intelligentsia who think they can govern better than him
Nicholas silently processes the embezzlement report — convinced all officials steal, decides to send them to the ranks, yet knows this changes nothing; performs his punitive duty mechanically
Chernyshov credits Nicholas's plan for Hadji Murad's surrender; Nicholas accepts the flattery despite the plan being Ermolov's and contrary to his own 1845 strategy
Tolstoi's authorial digression exposing Nicholas's self-delusion: continual flattery has severed his perception from reality and logic
Nicholas reviews the case of Bzhezovski, a Polish-descent student who stabbed a professor; Nicholas's ethnic prejudice against Poles surfaces immediately

Nicholas sentences Polish student Bzhezovski to 12×1000 gauntlet strokes — knowing it is fatal torture — while congratulating himself on having abolished capital punishment; reveals his self-deluding cruelty
Nicholas orders Chernyshov to continue the policy of destroying Chechen dwellings and food supplies via raids — confirms scorched-earth strategy remains unchanged

Nicholas orders Uniate peasants who resist conversion to run the gauntlet (de facto death sentence) and punishes a newspaper editor by conscripting him — casual exercise of absolute power on domestic matters.
Bibikov inwardly recognises the injustice of Nicholas's orders but suppresses dissent to protect his forty-year career — silent complicity as systemic mechanism.
Nicholas orders a pension paid to the mother of 'yesterday's girl' — a transactional gesture after what is implied to be exploitation, cloaked in imperial munificence.

At the New Year dinner, Baron Lieven and the Prussian Ambassador discuss disquieting news from Poland — referencing the political context established in the previous chunk (Nicholas moving two divisions to the Prussian frontier).
Baron Lieven and the Prussian Ambassador discuss Poland and Caucasus as Russia's two festering wounds, each requiring ~100,000 men — ironic framing of imperial overreach before Nicholas enters
Prussian Ambassador privately mocks Nicholas's 'strategic ability' while publicly flattering him — exposes the hollowness of imperial court validation
Nicholas attends ballet, singles out a dancer, orders diamond ring delivered — casual imperial lust juxtaposed with war decisions
Chapter XVI begins: January 1852 raid on Chechnya ordered — four infantry battalions, two Cossack companies, eight guns march out
Butler leads the 5th Company at the column's head, filled with romantic excitement — joy of life, danger, and desire for action.
Butler's company pursues retreating Chechens into a ravine and discovers a Chechen aoul
Russian soldiers burn the aoul's corn, hay, and saklyas — deliberate destruction of civilian infrastructure
Butler's company raids and burns a deserted Chechen aoul — corn, hay, and saklyas torched, fowl seized; officers lunch in the smoke.
Major begins a comparison between Caucasus service and Petersburg life (chunk truncated mid-sentence).
Arrival at fort; Marya Dmitrievna prepares domestic welcome (pie, cabbage soup) for Petrov
Marya Dmitrievna hosts dinner at the fort; Major Petrov drinks himself insensible; Butler sleeps contentedly
Chechen aoul inhabitants confront destroyed fountain and polluted mosque; Mullah and assistants cleanse the mosque
Villagers deliberate: rebuild under threat of renewed Russian raids or submit to Russians; old men pray and unanimously send envoys to Shamil for help
Hadji Murad greets Butler with 'Kotkildy'/'Saubul' exchange — first interpersonal contact establishing the kunak friendship thread.
Butler finds front door locked, orderlies absent drinking; navigates to kitchen to find Marya Dmitrievna baking — domestic chaos contrasts with the gravity of HM's arrival
Major Petrov wakes hungover, grumbles about authorities sending 'that devil' to him, calls for vodka as medicine before facing Hadji Murad
Shamil returns to Vedeno on Jan 6, 1852 after ambiguous battle — Russians say he fled, he claims victory — greeted with triumphant celebration

Shamil makes a theatrical triumphal entry into the aoul riding a white Arab steed, deliberately wearing plain garments while his murids wear gold and silver — a calculated display of austere power that commands absolute reverence from the crowd.
Shamil returns to Vedeno (Dargo-Vedin) after a failed campaign — publicly declared victorious but privately knows it was unsuccessful: Chechen aouls burned, border tribes wavering toward the Russians
Shamil crosses the outer court crowded with petitioners and supplicants, receiving prostrated salutes; suppresses all attention and enters inner court
Shamil's internal conflict: exhausted, wants rest and Aminal's company, yet must perform midday prayers as religious leader — duty vs. human desire
Prisoners glimpsed by narrator/implied observer: dirty, exhausted, embittered, hating one another — contrasted with free mounted warriors singing 'Lya illyah il Allah'
Jemal Eddin drafts and Shamil signs a proclamation forbidding Chechens from surrendering to the Russians, invoking religious duty and historical memory of the 1840 disarmament.
Shamil's council deliberates and concludes they must act through Hadji Murad's son Yusuf to pressure HM into returning
Shamil performs prophetic silence ritual before pronouncing sentence — establishing divine authority for coercive ultimatum
Shamil delivers ultimatum to Yusuf: HM returns before Bairam or family is dispersed, Yusuf is blinded
Yusuf's ambivalence revealed — he admires Shamil, does not fully share father's hostility; political and generational fracture within HM's family
Shamil revises threat mid-sentence: not death but blinding — 'as I do to all traitors' — escalating psychological cruelty
Shamil commutes Yusuf's death sentence to blinding — a calculated act of mercy-as-cruelty that publicly brands Hadji Murad a traitor
Yusuf, upon leaving Shamil's presence, seizes a guard's dagger and attempts suicide; restrained and returned to the pit — signals his psychological breaking point
Shamil seeks out wife Aminal after evening prayers; she deliberately avoids him, punishing him for the silk gift to Zeidat — brief domestic comedy undercuts his absolute power
Butler and Hadji Murad develop genuine friendship across language barrier — Eldar's welcoming smile signals HM's approval of Butler
Eldar's warm reception of Butler signals Hadji Murad's genuine affection for him — a rare crack of personal warmth in HM's guarded exterior
Hadji Murad gifts his white burka to Marya Dmitrievna — a formal gesture of reciprocal hospitality, cementing kunak bond with Butler's household
Butler's farewell exchange ('Kunak bulug') — emotional punctuation on the Russian-Chechen friendship thread
Hadji Murad gifts his burka to Marya Dmitrievna as gesture of respect after she praises it; she wishes him success in rescuing his son
Arslan Khan (Kumukh prince, blood-feud with HM) arrives and draws pistol; Hadji Murad rushes down porch like a cat despite lameness — assassination attempt cut short by HM's reflexes
Prince Baryatinsky arrives as new left-flank commander and musters a large expedition toward Kurin, continuing Tsar's campaign orders via Chernyshov
Baryatinsky's open affair with Simon Vorontsov's wife Marya Vasilevna is common knowledge among officers and soldiers — social scandal within the military aristocracy

Marya Vasilevna stays at the camp; her presence with Baryatinsky is an open secret causing soldiers to be placed on night ambush duty, generating resentment among officers and men not in the aristocratic circle
Butler visits the camp on leave, reconnects with old comrades Poltoratsky and others, is welcomed warmly and invited to Vorontsov's dinner
Farewell dinner for Kozlovsky: Baryatinsky toasts him; Kozlovsky delivers a tearful, stammering speech of gratitude to his fellow officers before departing the regiment.
Butler drifts to Poltoratsky's quarters and joins a card game; he begins gambling with money he cannot afford, foreshadowing personal dissolution.
Butler wakes at noon, faces reality of 470-ruble debt; begins writing to brother to beg a loan
Butler attempts to borrow 500 rubles from his brother, a stingy relative, and the major to repay a 470-ruble debt to a stranger — all avenues uncertain or closed.
Narrator establishes the epistemological gap: for Russians the episode is a lucky Caucasian-war event; for Hadji Murad it is a terrible life crisis.

Hadji Murad's family crisis recapped: Shamil holds family prisoner in Vedeno, threatening to blind/kill son and distribute women to aouls; Hadji Murad has devised a plan to rescue them via Avar loyalists for 3000 rubles
Avar loyalists in Daghestan plan to intercept the family if Shamil moves them from Vedeno; rescue only possible en route, not inside Vedeno
Hadji Murad's friends in the mountains definitively refuse to rescue his family, citing fear of Shamil's threatened tortures — the last external option for a negotiated family rescue collapses
By midnight Hadji Murad resolves to flee to Vedeno with loyal Avars, break in, and either die or rescue his family — the decision that triggers the escape sequence
Hadji Murad resolves to flee to the mountains, break into Vedeno, and rescue his family from Shamil — even at the cost of his life. Decision whether to return to Russians or fight Shamil is deferred.
Gamzalo's internal state revealed: he has wanted to kill Russians and return to the hills from the very beginning; Hadji Murad's order finally aligns with his own desire — satisfaction.
All four murids spend the rest of the night checking weapons in preparation for dawn escape.
Gamzalo rouses the four murids; all night they clean rifles, pistols, sharpen swords and daggers — covert preparation for escape attempt.
Hadji Murad performs morning ablutions and waits for permission to ride out, unable to leave without officer's approval — gilded cage made explicit
Khanefi's song triggers memory of Hadji Murad's mother — young, strong, carrying him through mountains as a child
Memory cascades to Yusuf — his shaved head, his physical vitality, his pride when asked to stay behind and protect the women, his last farewell at the stream
Hadji Murad confronts the image of Shamil threatening to blind Yusuf — cannot bear to think of what will be done to his wife; jumps up and calls Eldar
Hadji Murad's anguish over Shamil's threat to blind Yusuf and harm his wife drives him from stillness to action — he calls Eldar and demands horses to ride.
Butler's moral decline accelerates: debts postponed via usurer, daily drinking, and a failed gross advance on Marya Dmitrievna.
Baryatinsky's detachment arrives at the fort for a planned advance through Chechnya; officers' dinner degenerates into a drinking bout with Major Petrov sword-fighting imaginary foes.
Major Petrov is drunk and delusional, hacking at imaginary foes and singing a song mocking Shamil — comic degradation of military romanticism
Butler leaves the drunken scene, walks home under moonlight; encounters Marya Dmitrievna and nearly resumes his advances before being interrupted
Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev arrives with an escort, bringing unspecified news — dramatic arrival suggests plot development incoming
Kamenev arrives at the fort at night with Hadji Murad's severed head in a sack — the physical proof of HM's death and failed escape
The head is displayed in moonlight; Hadji Murad's face still shows a 'kindly childlike expression' despite the mortal wounds — Tolstoi's final humanising image of HM
Marya Dmitrievna turns away in silence and retreats inside — wordless moral revulsion at imperial trophy violence
Butler recognises the head as belonging to his kunak — personal grief collides with imperial triumphalism
Kamenev states HM 'wanted to give us the slip, but was caught' — offhand bureaucratic dismissal of a heroic death
Kamenev arrives at Butler's outpost carrying Hadji Murad's severed head in a sack — the immediate aftermath of HM's death, seen through Butler's horrified recognition
The drunken major and officers inspect and eulogize Hadji Murad's head ('He was a fine fellow / a valiant head') — grotesque tribute that underscores imperial moral bankruptcy
Marya Dmitrievna condemns the display as barbarism ('Cut-throats, really') — sole voice of moral clarity in the scene, then exits in disgust
Kamenev reveals his mission: to carry the head around to forts and aouls as official imperial spectacle — the state's final instrumentalization of HM's corpse
Butler asks Kamenev to recount how HM died — sets up retrospective account of the escape attempt and killing (Chapter XXV begins)
Hadji Murad deliberately defies order limiting escort to fewer henchmen — brings all five on April 25 ride; commander does not insist, yielding to HM's feigned deafness
Cossack escort introduced — five men, each briefly characterized (Nazarov leads); dramatic irony: reader senses these will be HM's final adversaries
Idyllic spring landscape described as HM rides out — foliage, grass, corn, river in sunshine; pastoral beauty contrasts with the impending violence
HM accelerates pace beyond fort; henchmen and Cossacks forced to trot to keep up — first sign of HM seizing control of the situation
Ferapontov identified as the man who sold gunpowder to Gamzalo — retroactive confirmation of Gamzalo's weapons access; potential earlier conspiracy hint
Hadji Murad accelerates his horse away from the Cossack escort, signaling the beginning of his escape attempt
Nazarov pursues Hadji Murad at full gallop, riding in naive exhilaration — unaware of the mortal danger
Hadji Murad draws his pistol and shoots Nazarov as Nazarov reaches for his bridle — the escape turns violent
Hadji Murad's murids engage the remaining Cossacks — Ignatov's horse falls and crushes his leg, murids hack at him; Petrakov about to rush in
Nazarov's horse careens wildly after he is hit; Ignatov's horse falls, crushing his leg — two mountaineers hack him with swords; Petrakov rushes to help but is shot in back and side, falls dead.
Mishkin breaks free and gallops toward the fortress; Khanefi and Bata pursue but cannot catch him — he escapes to raise the alarm.
Gamzalo finishes off Ignatov and slashes Nazarov; Bata strips the slain Cossacks of cartridge-pouches; Khanefi wants Nazarov's horse but HM orders him to leave it.
HM and murids ride hard — already six miles from Nukha when the fort tower fires the alarm shot.
Fort commander learns of escape, cries out in despair ('They've done for me!'). Full pursuit mobilised: Cossacks, militia from pro-Russian aouls, 200+ horsemen, 1000-ruble reward offered.
HM halts his sweating horse miles down the road, turns LEFT toward the river instead of right toward the mountains — tactical misdirection to deceive pursuers.
Hadji Murad makes a tactical feint — turns left (toward Alazan River) instead of right (toward mountains) to deceive pursuers
Rice-field flooded as bog; horses exhaust themselves unable to cross; party forced to shelter in shrubs until nightfall
Nightingales singing in the shrubs trigger HM's memory of the Hamzad song — foreshadowing his own imminent death
Hadji Murad performs ablutions and prays — spiritual preparation; sound of approaching horses ends the peace
Hadji Murad performs ablutions — a final act of Muslim devotion before battle, invoking the memory of Hamzad
Karganov's militia surrounds the thicket after an old man betrays HM's location — encirclement complete
Hadji Murad orders his men to dig entrenchments in a ditch — defiant last stand begins
Militia commander calls on HM to surrender; HM's men open fire, killing the commander's horse — resistance declared
Gamzalo's horse shot in the head — HM's group begins to take losses in the siege
Gamzalo's horse is hit but does not fall — bloods the grass; murids hold entrenchment with precision fire, wounding three militiamen who cannot advance
Hadji Aga of Mekhtuli arrives with ~200 reinforcements; his presence as a former kunak now turned enemy sharpens the betrayal motif at the climax
Akhmet Khan — son of Hadji Murad's old enemy — arrives alongside Hadji Aga, compressing personal and political enmity into one assault wave
Hadji Aga calls for surrender; HM answers with a shot — last refusal of capitulation; sword charge ordered, mass assault begins
Individual murid characterisation under fire: Gamzalo exults, Khan Mahoma sings and fires leisurely, Eldar trembles to charge, Khanefi loads methodically — each voice-profile crystallised in extremis
HM considers a mounted break for the river — the last strategic option in his mind before the assault overwhelms
Eldar fires recklessly, desperate to charge the enemy with his sword, exemplifying youthful fearless loyalty to Hadji Murad.
Khanefi serves as loader under fire, methodically passing ammunition — steady, selfless, servant even in extremis.
Bata is first wounded — bullet in neck; continues fighting while spitting blood and swearing.
Hadji Murad takes his first wound in the shoulder; plugs it with cotton wool from his beshmet and fights on.
Eldar is shot dead while rising to charge; his ram-eyes gaze at Hadji Murad one last time — intimate, wordless death.
Khanefi, undaunted, strips ammunition from Eldar's dead body and continues loading.
Khan Mahoma sings while firing — his song a defiant counterpoint to the closing Russian cordon.
Hadji Murad takes fatal wound in left side; knows he is dying and plugs it mechanically.
Dying, Hadji Murad experiences rapid-fire memory images: Abu Nutsal Khan (severed cheek), Vorontsov (soft cunning voice), Yusuf, Sofiat, Shamil (half-closed eyes) — all without pity or anger.
Hadji Murad rises wounded from the ditch, fires his pistol killing one attacker, and charges the enemy with his dagger despite mortal wounds — the final act of defiance
Hadji Murad falls, collapses against a tree, then drops dead like a mown thistle — the physical climax of his death
Hadji Aga decapitates the corpse; Karganov, Hadji Aga, Akhmet Khan and militiamen gather around the bodies in triumph; Khan Mahoma, Khanefi, and Gamzalo are taken prisoner
Author-narrator closes the frame: the nightingales resume singing; the crushed thistle in the ploughed field is named as the image that prompted the entire story — thistle-frame closure
Nightingales resume singing after the silence of gunfire — nature reclaims the moment of death, closing the battle's acoustic frame.
Author's final frame: the crushed thistle in the ploughed field — the opening image returns as explicit epitaph for Hadji Murad's death, closing the narrator's bracket.

themes: Absolute power enforces conformity through careerism — Bibikov's silence mirrors every subordinate's capitulation to Nicholas
Absolute power is porous — Shamil can condemn armies but cannot command a wife's attention
Agency vs. paralysis: Hadji Murad devises plans but cannot act directly
Aksinya mourns husband and self together — the war destroys domestic futures as well as lives
Anonymous soldiers die far from home while bureaucratic language ('defending his Tsar, his Fatherland, and the Orthodox Faith') sanitizes the loss
Anonymous soldiers die while the imperial machine erases and falsifies their deaths
Anonymous soldiers march and sing while Hadji Murad's fate is decided at imperial levels
Anonymous soldiers pay the price for imperial posturing — Avdeev wounded by casual, almost recreational firing ordered by Poltoratsky
Anonymous soldiers pay the price for imperial posturing — Avdeev's wound inflicted by casual military order, his death unremarked
Anti-imperial elegy: the crushed thing was alive, vital, irreducible
Anticipated encounter between worlds — Hadji Murad's mountain order vs. Russian imperial military bureaucracy
Autocratic self-delusion: power insulated from reality by flattery
Autocratic self-delusion: the Tsar literally cannot conceive that he has done wrong — his conscience is structurally blocked by narcissism
Baryatinsky's expedition and affair simultaneously illustrate how Russian imperial power operates: military brutality alongside aristocratic moral corruption
Betrayal by former kunak (Hadji Aga) as micro-image of the novel's macro-betrayal logic — loyalties are always provisional
Betrayal by the ordinary — an unnamed old man, not a soldier, delivers HM to his executioners
Blinding as punishment for treason — symbolic erasure of the traitor's witness, more cruel than death
Blood feud as inexorable law — law no one chose, everyone must obey
Blood vengeance as sacred duty — 'where the forelegs have gone the hind legs must follow'
Blood-feud as inescapable death-shadow that follows HM even among allies
Body's refusal of comfort: Avdeev's gut rejects vodka — the body knows what the mind won't admit (mortal wound)
Bureaucratic anxiety beneath imperial triumph — Vorontsov's private doubt undercuts public victory
Bureaucratic betrayal: Klugenau's absence enables Akhmet Khan's persecution — institutional failure as personal doom
Bureaucratic deferral as a form of power — Vorontsov's 'I will think it over' traps Hadji Murad without refusing him
Bureaucratic delay as imperial weapon — Vorontsov's deferral mirrors Nicholas I's stalling, trapping HM between two empires
Bureaucratic delay vs. urgent personal stakes — Vorontsov's deliberate unhearing of the family-ransom plea shows imperial machinery indifferent to human urgency
Bureaucratic humiliation vs. warrior dignity — Hadji Murad's slap is the only power left to him
Bureaucratic machinery preparing its report — war reduced to paperwork and posture
Bureaucratic risk-calculus versus genuine trust: Vorontsov frames the decision as institutional rather than personal
Bureaucratic violence: war decisions flow from dinner table to broken men and horses
Butler's Caucasus illusion persisting but eroding — he still seeks poetry in warfare but feels pity instead
Butler's friendship with HM is the human cost of empire — personal grief vs. institutional triumph
Butler's romantic Caucasus illusion deepened by alcohol and collective emotion
Butler's romantic illusion of war — the aesthetic excitement of the soldier who has not yet seen the cost
Butler's romantic projection onto mountain life — the outsider who romanticizes what he cannot truly inhabit; ironic counterpoint to HM's actual suffering
Butler's romantic self-congratulation as moral blindness — beauty of landscape masking brutality just enacted
Butler's romanticism of war exposed: he savors the thrill of danger while participating in atrocity
Captivity within gilded bureaucracy — cannot ride without officer's permission, officer is asleep
Cheerful proximity to death — officers joke and drink while bullets fly
Chivalric code of the mountain warrior contrasted with drawing-room gossip
Circular structure: opening frame closes, death becomes meaning
Civilisation as performance: the drawing room enacts imperial normalcy over an abyss of mutual suspicion
Civilizational suspicion masked by courtly ritual — the dinner scene externalises paranoia without a word of accusation
Class and patronage vs. rank and merit — family connections versus years of service
Class hierarchy enforced through casual violence (threat to strike, then withdrawal)
Class stratification in the Russian military: 'aristocratic set' vs. everyone else
Coercion through family as instrument of imperial/theocratic power — mirrors Nicholas I's use of state machinery
Collateral deaths (Nazarov, Ignatov, Petrakov) — ordinary soldiers pay price for their commanders' politics
Collateral devastation of imperial warfare — innocents bear the cost of Hadji Murad's political choice
Comic relief as character exposition — Khan Mahoma's winking irreverence vs. Gamzalo's rigid piety
Complicit pursuers — the twenty men chase only to perform loyalty to Shamil, not from conviction
Compulsory serf labor (carting bricks) juxtaposed with compulsory military service: both systems grind the peasant body.
Confession as political negotiation — Hadji Murad's story is simultaneously personal truth and tactical positioning
Confinement dressed as hospitality — the guest chamber is a polite prison
Conscription as social death — 'a soldier was a severed branch'
Contrast between imperial coldness in public and private appetite in secret
Contrast with Hadji Murad's predicament: HM is trapped by forces beyond him; Nicholas traps others by will
Costume as autobiography — HM's turban carries the history of his persecution by Akhmet Khan and Klugenau, worn as silent defiance
Court intrigue and personal enmity masquerading as state policy
Court jealousy (Chernyshov vs. Vorontsov) mirrors tribal rivalry in the mountains — power corrupts at every elevation
Cross-cultural charm: HM's blush at Marya Vasilevna's hand — vulnerability beneath the warrior legend
Cross-cultural encounter: two worlds meet on a dusty fort street
Cultural incomprehension as unbridgeable wall — HM's polite non-answer to the half-naked women encapsulates the entire Russian-Chechen contact zone
Cultural relativism — each nation's customs are self-validating (dog-and-ass proverb)
Death and mortality creeping into Nicholas's consciousness — brother's recent death surfaces unbidden
Death as conversion: the dying murid's last words as turning point
Death as spectacle: the state circuits the head as a control mechanism over the population
Death does not belong to the body — HM's last sensation is disconnection from flesh
Death strips away all affect — neither hatred nor love survive the fatal moment
Death with dignity versus war's indignity: Avdeev's grace contrasts with Poltoratsky's breezy 'how goes it, my lad'
Death with dignity — HM chooses to fight like Hamzad rather than surrender, completing the Hamzad identification begun in his life story
Debt and dependency as universal trap — Butler's financial paralysis rhymes with HM's political paralysis
Decisive solitude — the critical moment is interior, wordless, nocturnal; action follows inner resolution
Defiant song against annihilation — Khan Mahoma's singing as anti-imperial gesture
Dehumanization as the engine of colonial violence — both sides cease to see the other as human
Desire as distraction — Poltoratsky's infatuation mirrors the Russian military's blindness to real stakes
Dignity and defiance at the point of annihilation — HM's unerring aim as moral stance, not merely military skill
Dignity in surrender — Hadji Murad's bearing is imposing even in submission
Diplomatic contact as threshold moment — the defection process begins at night, clandestinely
Discipline and restraint as warrior identity — HM eats a mouthful after 24 hours of fasting
Dissent is structurally impossible: Chernyshov's caution, Bibikov's private horror — both swallowed whole
Distrust codified in alliance — two enemies bind themselves out of necessity
Domestic life continues indifferent to imperial sacrifice — the farm knows nothing of the war
Domestic life of the colonial officer class as counterpoint to HM's fugitive exhaustion
Domestic normalcy (pie, soup) as counterpoint to military brutality — Marya Dmitrievna as anchor
Domesticity as ironic frame for geopolitical encounter
Dullness and existential drift as the soldier's true enemy
Eagles dwell in mountains — the murid's proud retort signals that honour survives defection
Empire as social theatre — Vorontsov's palace dinner trivializes HM's world-altering surrender to a courier dispatch
Encirclement as imperial metaphor — Russia closes around the last free Chechen from every direction
Escape and reinvention: the Caucasus as refuge from civilian failure (gambling debt)
Ethnic persecution: anti-Polish prejudice as reflex of imperial guilt
Faith and discipline as warrior identity — prayer maintained even in flight
Faith as inner fortress: prayer before receiving bad news
Family as Hadji Murad's hidden wound — the note establishes his central vulnerability
Family as hostage — the personal cost of political defection made explicit
Family as irreducible moral anchor — all strategic calculation is overridden by the duty to rescue his family
Family as political hostage — love weaponised by power
Family as political hostage — the personal cost of Hadji Murad's defection made concrete
Family as political weapon: Shamil's plan to use HM's son as bait is the inverse of HM's own desperate love for his family
Family as the only chain that binds HM; his logic is entirely transactional with the Russians
Family as the ultimate hostage — HM's paralysis and explosion of motion mirrors the impossibility of his position
Family as the ultimate vulnerability — the hostage-bond that imprisons even the unconquerable
Family grief displaced as domestic anger — the cost of empire felt in the kitchen, not just the battlefield.
Family separation haunts even the fantasy — the dream includes Shamil's wives weeping, echoing HM's own captive family
Fatalism as dignity — HM's smile and Allah's-will shrug transforms mortal threat into character revelation
Fate and chance in war: 'Each man get what fate sends'
Fate closing in — tactical brilliance cannot overcome terrain and numbers
Fate vs. agency — spring beauty and innocent Cossacks create dramatic irony; audience knows what characters do not
Father-son mirroring: Hadji Murad admires in Yusuf the daring he himself embodies
Female social power operating outside formal military hierarchy
First impressions vs. legend — the fearsome warrior reveals a disarming human smile
First seeds of HM vs. Shamil antagonism planted here
Flattered vanity as political lever: Loris-Melikov instrumentalises HM's pride to extract information
Flattery as systemic corruption of governance
Flight and pursuit as existential condition — Hadji Murad is perpetually hunted, never safe
Flight as assertion of will — HM chooses defection over submission even under fire
Flogging scars as palimpsest: the empire has always written violence on peasant bodies
Forgiveness and selflessness as the only freedom available to the powerless — Avdeev's dying message releases his brother from guilt
Freedom pursued at lethal cost — HM's break confirms he values death over captivity
Freedom vs. captivity: Yusuf's imprisonment made unbearable by the sound of celebration he cannot join
Freedom vs. captivity: prisoners contrasted with mounted warriors — envy as motif
Gamzalo as uncaged threat — red eye fixed on horse ears, contempt coded in body language
Gender and power: Marya Dmitrievna's domestic authority cuts through military bureaucracy
Gender seclusion as lived cultural code — not commented on, simply enacted
Generational division: Yusuf's veneration of Shamil vs. HM's existential opposition — loyalty shaped by what one has personally suffered
Gift economy as language of honor — burka to Marya Dmitrievna signals mutual respect across enemy lines
Gift exchange as cross-cultural dignity ritual — burka and sword transcend language
Gilded cage: gifts (the watch) signal ownership, not generosity; HM checking the hour underscores his need to reclaim agency within captivity
God as the only court above geopolitics — HM's fatalism as dignity
Gold sewn into cloth: material survival under imperial captivity
Grief embodied physically: mother tears her face, grandfather cuts a stick in silent endurance
Grotesque eulogies: the officers' drunken admiration ('fine fellow', 'gave me a sword') is both sincere and obscene — Tolstoi's anti-war irony at its sharpest
HM as political asset and liability simultaneously — his treatment signals Russian intent to all of Daghestan
HM's fate discussed at highest level without any understanding of who he actually is
HM's lameness vs. cat-like reflexes — physical paradox echoing his larger impossible position
HM's men as a mirror of HM: each murid a facet of his character (frivolous / iron / obedient / blood-bound)
HM's pride survives captivity: hand on dagger hilt, smile of contempt
HM's self-knowledge: he knows this is likely a death-ride, yet proceeds
HM's silent political intelligence — he reads power dynamics without language, through observation alone
Hadji Murad as legend vs. Hadji Murad as person — the gap between image and reality widens
Hadji Murad as pawn passed between officials, his agency suspended while Russians squabble
Hadji Murad as spectacle — his very presence in a room of petitioners signals how far he has fallen from independent warrior to supplicant
Hadji Murad as the one who sees the trap — his political intelligence established in backstory
Hadji Murad caught between two systems, both flawed
Hadji Murad surrounded yet purposeful — compressed danger breeds decisive action
Hadji Murad's agency curtailed step by step — from honoured guest to man in a waiting room
Hadji Murad's composed otherness: present but unreachable, a man between worlds
Hadji Murad's defection radiates outward: the Chechen pursuers' presence directly causes Avdeev's wound — HM's choice has mortal consequences for those around him
Hadji Murad's divided attention: the domestic warmth of Marya Dmitrievna vs. the dread carried by each spy report
Hadji Murad's fate controlled by imperial caprice, not justice or strategy
Hadji Murad's fate decided in an imperial antechamber he will never enter — the protagonist's life as bureaucratic line-item
Hadji Murad's legend precedes him even into the drawing rooms of his enemies
Hadji Murad's legend precedes him; his fame silences a room before he appears
Hadji Murad's surrender is used by Nicholas as propaganda; reality is escalation, not peace
Half-naked Russian women as civilizational marker — Tolstoi's ironic ethnography of imperial mores
Hamzad echo: the song of a doomed predecessor mirrors HM's own end
Hierarchy of loyalty: Eldar mirrors HM even in hunger, subordinating self entirely
Hierarchy of wakefulness: power stays lit while subordinates sleep
Hierarchy softened by ritual: the ceremony of farewell as social glue
Historical tide as personal fate — Muridism's spread is not ideology but the tide that drowned his world
Hollow ritual vs. genuine faith: prayers repeated without meaning mirror the empire's hollow forms of law and order
Honor as non-negotiable constraint — the spit incident explains everything
Honor codes in tension: Butler's restraint toward Marya mirrors Hadji Murad's code of dignity
Hospitality and farewell as ritual — the departure scene is staged as ceremony, not escape; underlines HM's trapped-guest status
Hospitality as mortal risk — Sado's loyalty costs him; kunak bond tested under pressure
Hospitality as performance — kunak gift custom weaponizes generosity as diplomatic shield
Human connection persists across enemy lines — Butler/HM friendship as counter-narrative to imperial enmity
Humiliation of dependency: Hadji Murad must beg for money owed to him by the Russians
Imperial bureaucracy as cage — summons to Tiflis further delays family rescue
Imperial bureaucracy as cage: Loris-Melikov wants to help but is legally constrained
Imperial bureaucracy as obstacle — chain of command subordinates the historic moment to petty ego
Imperial bureaucracy as slow death machine — the courier bleeds horses to deliver a document that may doom Hadji Murad
Imperial bureaucracy as theater — the waiting room stages the full spectrum of those who depend on Vorontsov's favor, underscoring power's corrupting reach
Imperial bureaucracy vs. warrior autonomy — Vorontsov's French letter reduces HM to an intelligence asset
Imperial bureaucracy: chain-of-command quarrel resolved by handshake while the subject of the quarrel sits excluded in the next room
Imperial bureaucratic disorder — the machinery that is supposed to receive a legendary warrior is drunk, absent, or baking pies
Imperial chain-of-command friction: Vorontsov's personal diplomacy collides with Meller-Zakomelsky's institutional authority
Imperial condescension — French spoken as private channel excluding HM
Imperial conformity enforced through mimicry — courtiers literally model their appearance on Nicholas's face
Imperial contempt for independent thought — Nicholas treats liberal discourse as a personal threat to his authority
Imperial court as myth-manufacturing machine — praise of the enemy serves the patron's vanity
Imperial dehumanization: the conqueror reduces the conquered to a trophy object
Imperial dilemma: trust vs. control — Russia cannot fully embrace or fully imprison HM without political cost
Imperial distraction: Poland unrest at dinner table mirrors the earlier Prussian frontier maneuver — imperial machinery responding to political fear while HM's fate is a bureaucratic footnote.
Imperial hypocrisy — Nicholas claims contradictory strategies as his own
Imperial indignity: a man of HM's stature reduced to waiting for coin from a fat civilian
Imperial loneliness: Nicholas's only comfort is the thought of his own indispensability — the isolation of absolute power
Imperial machine in motion — impersonal, orderly violence executing Nicholas's casual command
Imperial masculinity and the performance of courage under fire
Imperial mercy as transactional currency, not moral virtue
Imperial myth-making machine: atrocities laundered into heroism at dinner tables
Imperial narcissism — Nicholas constructs a reality where all success confirms his genius
Imperial paperwork as the real cage — orders reduce a warrior to an administrative category
Imperial power as arbitrary theater: Chernyshov's 'respectful amazement' at barbarism
Imperial power as performance — Nicholas stages his authority through physical space and ritual
Imperial power as sexual entitlement — Nicholas's authority over officers and women is identical in kind
Imperial power as solemn ceremony: Vorontsov's 'stern and solemn' face contrasts yesterday's social warmth
Imperial power reduces the heroic individual to a trophy object
Imperial privilege insulated from consequence — aristocrats' social theater continues while soldiers absorb its costs
Imperial privilege insulated from the violence it commands
Imperial self-deception: official history vs. lived truth — the Dargo campaign is a 'brilliant achievement' in reports, a catastrophe in memory
Imperial self-delusion: Nicholas mistakes flattery for strategic validation
Imperial social performance masking military reality — champagne and cards while Hadji Murad waits outside
Imperial social performance vs. private reality — Poltoratsky's ecstasy dissolves in a grimy billet
Imperial social spectacle as alienation — the opera and ball render Hadji Murad permanently Other
Imperial society as performance — dinner as stage where power and information are displayed
Imperial violence as routine: burning homes and crops is presented as unremarkable military procedure
Imperial violence reduced to butchery: decapitation as trophy, not justice
Impossible belonging — the falcon fable crystallises HM's statelessness: no side will accept him
Imprisonment as imperial instrument — both sides (Russian fort, Shamil's pit) cage those connected to Hadji Murad
Information as weapon — Jemal Eddin's report on HM's family plot converts domestic intelligence into political leverage
Information asymmetry as power — Marya Vasilevna already knows, forces Vorontsov to confirm
Information control: both Russians and Shamil use rumor and image as weapons
Inherited hatred: Patimat refuses to watch Shamil, mirroring HM's own enmity — family bond as political stance
Institutional cruelty masked by procedural language — gauntlet-as-mercy is Nicholas's signature self-delusion
Institutional failure of will: promise to brother and self broken instantly under social pressure — mirrors how every officer capitulates to the system above him

Justice as performance — the Shariat sentences (amputation, beheading) demonstrate Shamil's power before the pivotal HM decision

Kunak bond as the only genuine human connection amid imperial machinery
Kunak bond — Butler's farewell seals friendship as binding oath
Kunak hospitality as moral absolute — Sado risks death yet does not hesitate
Kunak-loyalty as moral counter-code to imperial hierarchy — Hadji Murad extends it sincerely, Russians receive it as exotic ceremony
Legend vs. human reality — the terrifying chief has a child's smile

Love suppressed — 'of what would be done to his wife Hadji Murad did not wish to think'
Loyalty and brotherhood — Osman dies at Hadji Murad's side
Loyalty as a transactional commodity — Shamil's betrayal mirrors the Russian system Hadji Murad now enters

Loyalty unto death: murids continue fighting even as comrades fall
Loyalty without certainty — Gamzalo praises the master he abandoned, exposing the complexity of allegiance
Loyalty without words — Gamzalo and Eldar act without being commanded, embodying the murid ethos of total devoted service

Martyrdom as the only honourable exit from an impossible position
Marya Dmitrievna's spontaneous defense of HM underscores the human connection that cuts across imperial categories
Marya Dmitrievna's wordless revulsion as the moral witness
Maternal sacrifice as the root of Hadji Murad's identity — his existence was purchased by his mother's blood
Memory and legend as consolation: the song transforms certain death into story
Memory as a double blade: the beauty of what was intensifies the horror of what may be

Mercy weaponized as cruelty: blinding as public stigma transcending death
Military failure masked by propaganda: Shamil's campaign failed but is declared victorious
Military romanticism hollowed out — Petrov's drunken sword-play against imaginary Shamil is grotesque parody of heroism
Misdirection as survival: HM turns left when all logic says right — the cunning of the cornered
Moonlit domestic scene contrasts with violence elsewhere — false peace before news arrives
Moonlit garden / nightingales contrast with covert military preparation — beauty against violence
Moral blindness at the apex of power — adultery and coercion don't register as wrongdoing
Moral corruption at the apex of empire — Tsar employs a known blackguard
Moral witness as counter-voice: Marya Dmitrievna's revulsion is the audience's proxy
Mortality of the common soldier vs. spectacle of the great man — Avdeev fades as HM arrives
Mother as origin of courage — the war song transfers the wound from mother to son symbolically
Mutual deception as diplomatic language — both parties know the other is lying and perform sincerity anyway
Mutual flattery as political currency: Chernyshov mirrors Nicholas, Nicholas mirrors himself
Narrative framing: the story told in safety contrasts with the extreme violence of the events described
Nature (gazelles) vs. military column: beauty and freedom fleeing before organised destruction
Nature (nightingales) counterpoints human violence (grinding blades)
Nature as indifferent witness and ominous chorus (nightingales, flooding bog)
Nature indifferent to human violence — life continues after death
Nature's indifference and renewal: nightingales resume the moment killing stops
Night as the domain of the hunted — stars, darkness, the hum from the mosque
Night as threshold: the ordinary watch becomes the hinge of history
Object as diplomatic gesture: the watch gift is an exchange of trust tokens across an unbridgeable cultural divide
Officers' detachment from suffering: Poltoratsky's breezy 'Tut, tut, tut' against Avdeev's agony
Oral history / storytelling as power: HM's life will reach the Tsar through his own words
Ordinary individuals crushed by imperial caprice (Bzhezovski)
Ordinary lives destroyed by empire — Petrakov as mother's-only-son echoes Avdeev; innocent soldiers ground between HM's necessity and Russian policy
Paranoid vigilance beneath social grace — HM's private face vs. public face
Patrician blindness — Vorontsov's snuff-taking pleasure and dismissal of Simon's fault reveals his insulation from consequence
Peasant grief is practical and brief: weep, return to work, hold a requiem, distribute holy bread

refocalize.scene: Thistle-plucking prologue
refocalize.recommended_pov: TRANSLATE_TO_VISUAL — open on extreme close-up of crimson thistle in ditch, hands (unseen owner) struggling to pluck it, flower destroyed; cut to title. No narrator. Thistle image recurs as motif when Hadji Murad is cornered. Implements NF-04 (cut authorial voice) + NF-08 (single frame use) + SC-06 (translate literary beauty to visual).

refocalize.scene: Author-Narrator's walk across ploughed field and thistle observation
refocalize.recommended_pov: DISSOLVE frame narrator entirely; open on VISUAL of mangled thistle in black field — camera as narrator — then CUT TO Hadji Murad riding into Makhmet at dusk

refocalize.scene: Authorial framing of Hadji Murad's past reputation ('famous for his exploits, who used never to ride out without his banner')
refocalize.recommended_pov: Translate into visual contrast — brief flash of HM in full regalia with banner and murids, then hard cut to cloaked fugitive on narrow street. No voice-over.

refocalize.scene: Old man's eagle/hare proverb and Mitchit raid report
refocalize.current_pov: Third-person omniscient narrator (Tolstoi) — old man's perspective and knowledge
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage through Hadji Murad's reception of the information — his stillness, his reading of the old man's evasion. Camera stays on HM's face, not the old man's. CF-08 / NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Sado's arrival and prayer ritual
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator describing Sado's physical appearance and ritual in detail
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's POV — we see what he scrutinizes: Sado's face, the door, the sounds outside. HM's watchful tension (listening for danger) drives the staging rather than narrator description. → CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Sado's daughter enters and is described at length by the narrator
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator (external physical description of the girl)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's arrested attention — his sudden silence when the women enter can be filmed as his POV registering her, making the gendered code visceral and personal rather than descriptive. NF-04 (cut omniscient narrator voice, translate to protagonist behavior/camera).

refocalize.scene: Women serving and departing the saklya
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator observing women's movements and Eldar's statue-like stillness
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's restrained gaze — he does not look at them either; camera stays on his face and hands, registering the cultural code without explanation. [CF-08, NF-04]

refocalize.scene: Chapter II opening — Russian patrol to the ambush plane tree
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator following Panov and the three Russian soldiers
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as counterpoint POV (parallel cut with Sado scene) rather than eliminating — the spatial irony of the two worlds sharing one dark night is load-bearing for the anti-imperial theme. Frame via intercut rather than full POV transfer. If a single-POV policy is enforced for the film, restage as Hadji Murad hearing distant Russian boot-sounds / firelight from the fort visible through a shutter crack (CF-08, NF-09).

refocalize.scene: Night ambush outpost — soldiers' idle gossip and smoking
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator following Panov and Avdeev
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as-is — Avdeev's POV anchors the common-soldier counterweight to HM's story; restage as Avdeev-centric scene to preserve his voice-profile introduction

refocalize.scene: Russian soldiers' night-watch conversation at the plane tree grove
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator / Russian soldiers (Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as contrast scene — but frame through HM's awareness if possible (e.g., HM hears Russian voices/jackals from a distance), or keep as inter-cut to establish the human cost on the Russian side without losing HM's line. Per CF-08: refocalize non-protagonist POVs as events HM perceives or later learns of. SC-01 test: scene passes on theme (imperial toll) and filmability, so KEEP with light reframe.

refocalize.scene: Night ambush outpost — soldiers' gossip and Avdeev's homesickness monologue

refocalize.scene: Scouts' delivery to Vorontsov's house and the colonel's still-burning light

refocalize.scene: Khan Mahoma's verbal report of Vorontsov's reception
refocalize.current_pov: Narrator relaying dialogue between envoys and Hadji Murad
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as-is — already focalized through Hadji Murad receiving information; no refocalization needed

refocalize.scene: Russian officers' mess discussion of Sleptsov + skirmish begins
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator / Russian officer collective (Poltoratsky focus)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as event Hadji Murad learns of secondhand — OR compress to a montage beat showing the Russian advance that HM's scouts report back to him; CF-08 applies

refocalize.scene: Chechen horsemen across the ravine watching Hadji Murad meet the Russians
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator / Poltoratsky's line of sight
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as Hadji Murad learning later (via murid report or his own glance) that his pursuers witnessed his crossing — converts background detail into HM's awareness of being observed and hunted even in Russian territory. CF-08 / NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Poltoratsky riding up and ordering fire without tactical necessity
refocalize.recommended_pov: Can stay as-is — Poltoratsky's POV here is not load-bearing for HM's arc, but the Avdeev consequence IS. Keep Avdeev-centric framing; Poltoratsky reduced to functional commander role, not POV anchor.

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov rides to meet HM / announces surrender to Poltoratsky
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage from Hadji Murad's POV — he approaches the Russian line; we see the fort, the sharpshooters, Poltoratsky's incomprehension. His smile is the scene's emotional anchor and it plays stronger as his conscious choice than as something Poltoratsky witnesses. CF-08 / NF-09

refocalize.scene: Avdeev refuses vodka / is carried to the fort
refocalize.recommended_pov: Can remain off-HM; but in film compress to a single cutaway before HM's arrival — SC-02 (dual-function: establishes mortality + clears stage for HM entrance)

refocalize.scene: Poltoratsky's surprised observation of HM's childlike smile and demeanor
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage through HM's eyes — what does HM read in the Russian officer's face? The refocalization preserves the surprise-of-reputation beat while anchoring POV on protagonist.

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov's interior anxiety about Meller-Zakomelsky jurisdictional breach
refocalize.recommended_pov: CUT or compress to single beat visible through HM's eyes — HM notices Vorontsov's smile falter, reads the politics without understanding the bureaucracy; CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Marya Vasilevna's drawing-room impression of Hadji Murad (her POV: his blush, his smile, her inclination toward him)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage through Hadji Murad's eyes: the strange pale world of Russian domesticity, the child, the unfamiliar rituals — same scene, protagonist as witness, no loss of dramatic content

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov retreats to his office to write reports — narrator follows him, leaving HM alone
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut the office scene; convey report-writing via a brief line of dialogue or visual — stay with Hadji Murad in the drawing room for continuity of protagonist POV (CF-08)

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov-Marya Vasilevna French dialogue about the dagger
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator reporting both sides of French exchange
refocalize.recommended_pov: Stay on Hadji Murad's face — he cannot understand French; film shows exclusion through HM's uncomprehending stillness, not through translated subtitles. → CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Hadji Murad's private room — checks on murids via Eldar
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep in HM's POV — fears shown through behavior (checking dagger, sitting armed) not narrated. Tolstoi's explicit 'he feared everything: that he might be seized...' becomes visual action. → NF-04, NF-09

refocalize.scene: Meller-Zakomelsky separates Hadji Murad and sends him to the waiting room
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage from Hadji Murad's POV — he is ushered away, left alone, hears Russian voices behind a closed door. His isolation and the reversal from honoured guest to detained subject land harder when experienced through his eyes. CF-08 / NF-09.

refocalize.scene: Marya Vasilevna's observation about the poisoning fear at dinner
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep beat but anchor it in HM's observable action (choosing his portion) — camera records the act, Vorontsov and Marya react. Removes need for her explanatory line; visual subtext suffices. NF-04 (cut authorial comment), SC-06 (translate inner fear to visual).

refocalize.scene: Jurisdictional quarrel in Meller-Zakomelsky's study
refocalize.current_pov: Third-person omniscient alternating between Vorontsov and Meller-Zakomelsky
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad waiting in the anteroom — intercut with muffled argument heard through the door; his stillness and powerlessness foregrounded — CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Marya Vasilevna's laughing intervention
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator; Marya Vasilevna as active agent
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's POV — aide-de-camp's expression shift as laughter leaks from the study; HM reads the room without understanding the language — CF-08

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov-Meller-Zakomelsky reconciliation and decision
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's POV — he sits in the next room reading the muffled sounds and body language through a doorway or thin wall; the scene becomes his silent intelligence-gathering moment rather than the officers' domestic resolution. CF-08, NF-09.

refocalize.scene: Avdeev in the hospital ward
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as Avdeev's own POV (he is the de facto protagonist of this strand). The ward patients, the doctor's examination, the scars — all filtered through Avdeev's pain-narrowed consciousness. NF-09 applied to secondary protagonist strand.

refocalize.scene: Avdeev's death scene — entire chunk
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator / Avdeev (Tolstoi stays close to Avdeev throughout)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as Avdeev POV — this is one of the few scenes where camera SHOULD leave Hadji Murad. The parallel death (HM surrounded by enemies, Avdeev surrounded by comrades) gains power through intercutting. Recommend: intercut Avdeev death scene with HM's captivity/isolation in Tiflis as structural contrast, not refocalize away from it.
refocalize.deviation_note: CF-08 recommends refocal onto HM, but M-02 (thematic priority) overrides: Avdeev's death IS the anti-imperial theme made flesh. Cutting or refocalizing it collapses the film's moral argument. KEEP as parallel POV, use for intercutting.

refocalize.scene: Avdeev family farm threshing floor — Chapter VIII
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator observing Russian peasant home simultaneously with soldier's death
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as cross-cut counterpoint; optionally frame as what Avdeev imagines / hallucinates in his final moment — NF-09, CF-08

refocalize.scene: Official military dispatch read by narrator
refocalize.recommended_pov: Translate to visual: show dispatch being written/stamped by a clerk while Avdeev's body is being wrapped — NF-04, SC-06

refocalize.scene: Entire Avdeev family threshing-floor scene
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator following Russian peasant family at home
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as Avdeev's dying memory/hallucination (intercut with death-bed scene in Caucasus) so HM's story-world remains primary frame; OR condense to a single cutaway — letter arriving at home — reported to HM-world via Butler's observation. CF-08, NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Avdeev family quarrel and ruble dispatch
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator (Avdeev family, no HM presence)
refocalize.recommended_pov: REFOCAL — cut to Peter/Avdeev receiving the letter at the fort, restaging the family grief from his end; or DISSOLVE entirely and convey via Avdeev's deathbed letter-reading in earlier chunk

refocalize.scene: Entire Avdeev family home-front mourning sequence (letter sent, returned, requiem)
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator following the Avdeev peasant family in rural Russia
refocalize.recommended_pov: Reframe as consequence seen from the fort or compressed into a single scene: a comrade (Panov or Serogin) learning of the returned letter, collapsing the home-front POV into a soldier's reaction. Alternatively, cut to a visual insert — the returned envelope, a candle lit — with no dialogue. CF-08 / NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Aksinya's grief and inner secret joy
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator (Tolstoi irony — shows public vs. private Aksinya)
refocalize.recommended_pov: CUT entirely — Hadji Murad has no access to this scene, it is wholly outside his trajectory. If Avdeev's fate must register, compress into a single environmental beat (empty bunk, comrades' silence). CF-08, NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Michael Vorontsov receiving the courier and entering the dinner party
refocalize.recommended_pov: KEEP but refocalize — Vorontsov IS a protagonist-adjacent antagonist (historical anchor). Scene can remain from his POV as the film needs to establish the imperial receiving-end of HM's surrender. However, the biographical backstory paragraph should be cut (NF-04) and replaced with behavioral cues in the scene itself. CF-08 (non-HM POV acceptable because Vorontsov is established antagonist, not a random side-character).

refocalize.scene: Dinner-table anecdote about Hadji Murad at Pahlen's detachment and the Dargo 'rescue'
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator explaining Vorontsov's Dargo history and the room's collective awareness of the gaffe
refocalize.recommended_pov: Stay on Vorontsov's face — his internal reaction (pride to wounded shame) is visible; cut the authorial exposition entirely. Manana's intervention lands harder if we don't over-explain. [NF-04, CF-08]

refocalize.scene: Entire Tiflis dinner conversation about Hadji Murad
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator observing courtiers and Vorontsov
refocalize.recommended_pov: REFOCAL via CF-08: restage as scene HM later learns about (he is informed that the Russians have been praising him at dinner — this arrives as a report from a murid or spy, sharpening his awareness that he is being instrumentalized as a political symbol rather than treated as an ally)

refocalize.scene: General's interrupted story
refocalize.current_pov: Authorial omniscient — we see the general's embarrassment and the aides-de-camp's warning looks
refocalize.recommended_pov: COMPRESS into Marya Vasilevna's POV: her social maneuvering becomes the visible action, cutting the general's interiority entirely — NF-04 (authorial narrator dissolved into character behavior)

refocalize.scene: Entire Tiflis dinner myth-making sequence
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as news that reaches Hadji Murad second-hand (a murid reports what the Russians say about him); or compress to a single expository beat in Hadji Murad's next scene → CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Simon's letter read aloud / jurisdictional quarrel summary
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut entirely; Simon-Meller quarrel is cuttable thread (see plotline graph); its resolution has no bearing on HM's trajectory → SS-02, SC-01

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov reads Simon's letter and reacts with snuff-taking pleasure
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as scene HM is waiting and HEARS the aide-de-camp mention the letter from Simon — HM's awareness that his surrender is already being processed upstairs. Reframes bureaucratic comfort as HM's vulnerability. CF-08.

refocalize.scene: Waiting room tableau of petitioners
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's POV entering the room — his eyes moving across the petitioners, reading the social hierarchy of supplication he has now joined. More filmable and keeps viewer inside HM's perspective. NF-09.

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov's silent internal reading of Hadji Murad (narrator reports both men's inner states simultaneously)
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator shuttles between Vorontsov's thoughts and Hadji Murad's perception
refocalize.recommended_pov: Stay on Hadji Murad's face/eyes reading Vorontsov — cut to Vorontsov's micro-expression; let audience decode, not narrator explain. CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Tolstoi's authorial aside revealing Vorontsov had 'long forgotten' the 1839 Avaria events despite saying 'I know, I know'
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator exposing Vorontsov's dissemblance directly
refocalize.recommended_pov: Translate to visual irony: Vorontsov's too-quick 'I know' + slight hesitation in his eyes, legible to the audience without narrator intervention — NF-04

refocalize.scene: Mutual silent truth-telling via eye contact (narrator's interior commentary on both men's unspoken understanding)
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator ventriloquizing both characters' interior states simultaneously
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as close-up reaction shot sequence — HM's gaze holds, Vorontsov's glance slides away first — conveying the power dynamic without any voice-over — NF-04, CF-08

refocalize.scene: Italian opera attendance and ballroom party at Vorontsovs'
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator observing Tiflis society from outside with ironic ethnographic distance
refocalize.recommended_pov: Stay on Hadji Murad's POV throughout — his impassivity as active resistance, not passive indifference; camera stays close to his face reading the alien spectacle; NF-04 (auktorial narrator dissolved into protagonist behavior) + CF-08 (refocalization on Tier-1 protagonist)

refocalize.scene: First Vorontsov interview ending — referral to chief of staff
refocalize.current_pov: Neutral narrator reports bureaucratic deflection flatly
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's face/hands as Vorontsov speaks — register the non-answer landing; his family hostage declaration should play as the scene's emotional peak, not Vorontsov's vague promise; NF-09 applies

refocalize.scene: Ball as social spectacle — narrated from omniscient perspective observing all guests' behavior toward HM
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's POV exclusively — camera stays close on his face and eyes; the half-dressed women and Vorontsov's epaulets read as alien objects he is cataloguing, not as 'normal' society. His attempt to speak about his family becomes the only moment of interiority. CF-08, NF-09

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov arrives to record HM's life story for the Tsar
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator sets up the institutional frame before HM speaks
refocalize.recommended_pov: Open on HM already seated, Loris-Melikov mid-explanation — cut the authorial setup, let the situation speak. NF-04

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov's framing of the dictation session (notebook, translation, sending to the Emperor)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Stay in Hadji Murad's POV — his flattered pause, his decision to speak; Loris-Melikov's notebook visible only as HM notices it. CF-08, NF-09.

refocalize.scene: HM's memory of his mother showing him the scar
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator rendering HM's interior memory
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as HM's subjective flashback — close-up on child's hand touching the scar, no narrator voice. TRANSLATE_TO_VISUAL. NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Arrival of Murid missionaries beating stones — mass conversion of Chechens and Avars
refocalize.current_pov: Hadji Murad narrating to Loris-Melikov (retrospective, distanced)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as HM's direct memory: young HM watching from the palace walls as the Murids arrive below, framed through his eyes — converts an abstract historical statement into a concrete visual memory. NF-09, CF-08.

refocalize.scene: Hamzad sends envoys to the Khans demanding Ghazavat allegiance
refocalize.current_pov: HM narrating at one remove (reported speech within backstory)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Either compress to a single line in HM's dictation or restage as the envoy arriving at the palace while young HM watches — keeps it in his POV. CF-08.

refocalize.scene: Umma Khan's corruption by Russian officers (cards, wine, bad places)
refocalize.current_pov: Hadji Murad narrating retrospectively to Loris-Melikov
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as witnessed scene from Hadji Murad's POV — he watches Umma Khan's degradation helplessly, then physically removes him; converts oral report into visual scene with dramatic agency

refocalize.scene: Hamzad's humiliation of the Khans' Elders
refocalize.current_pov: Reported speech via Hadji Murad's narration
refocalize.recommended_pov: Brief visual insert (30 seconds) showing the Elders' return to Khunzakh with shaved faces and cakes — wordless, kinetic; then cut back to HM's reaction; CF-08 refokalisation

refocalize.scene: Bulach Khan sent as hostage to Hamzad
refocalize.recommended_pov: Can remain narrated if the Khansha is present in the scene as a listener or interlocutor — her emotional weight carries the beat without POV switch

refocalize.scene: Khansha shaming Abu Nutsal Khan into riding to Hamzad
refocalize.current_pov: Hadji Murad retrospective narration (he was present but the emotional core is the Khansha-Abu Nutsal exchange)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as HM's POV — he watches the shaming, recognizes the fatal dynamic, is powerless to stop it. Restage as HM in the doorway, witnessing the moment the Khansha speaks the bee-sting line. His face carries the dread. CF-08 / NF-09.

refocalize.scene: Hamzad's theatrical reception of Umma Khan (singing, shooting, caracoling)
refocalize.current_pov: HM's narration — he is present but observing Hamzad's performance directed at Umma Khan
refocalize.recommended_pov: Film through HM's eyes: he watches Hamzad perform honor-as-theater, reads the performance as false. His recognition of the trap is the dramaturgical point. CF-08.

refocalize.scene: Shamil cutting off Hadji Murad at Hamzad's tent
refocalize.current_pov: HM's retrospective narration (already his POV — no change needed)
refocalize.recommended_pov: KEEP as HM POV — this is already correctly focalized. The moment is the seed of the HM-Shamil antagonism. High filmability. NF-09.

refocalize.scene: Abu Nutsal Khan's massacre in Hamzad's tent
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's POV as direct witness — film the memory as fragmented flash-cuts from HM's perspective (running away, sound of shots, glimpsed carnage), then cut back to his face in the present. Avoids VO; keeps shame visceral. CF-08 / NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov observing murids in their room
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as HM returning from prayer and seeing Loris-Melikov emerge from the murid room — HM reads Gamzalo's shut face, understands his household is being studied. Refocalises on protagonist, adds dramatic irony.

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov visits murids for debate on Shamil/Mansur
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as scene Hadji Murad overhears from his adjacent bedroom — he would hear his men debate the legitimacy of the world he left behind, adding dramatic irony to his captive situation → CF-08, NF-09

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov profiles the murids — including reading Gamzalo as potential spy
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad — restage as HM watching Loris-Melikov watch Gamzalo; HM reads the Russian's suspicion and registers its danger. CF-08 applies: keep the information, shift the lens.

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov's interior monologue suspecting HM of espionage while observing Gamzalo
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as HM overhearing / sensing the Russian's suspicion — or cut the interior narration entirely and show suspicion via Loris-Melikov's body language in the drawing-room scene that follows. CF-08: refocalise onto HM as primary POV anchor.

refocalize.scene: Loris-Melikov's interrogation of Khanefi in the side room
refocalize.recommended_pov: Film can keep this scene — Loris-Melikov POV is acceptable here as it delivers Khanefi's backstory efficiently. Alternatively, HM could relay Khanefi's story himself in the Chapter XIII narration session, collapsing both scenes.

refocalize.scene: Hamzad kills the Khansha via Aseldar
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator / HM recounting — HM was not present
